Name: 37_english_translation_translate_language
Representative_Docs: ['context is important here at my uni it is a big no no in academic english courses for obvious reasons the course objective is to begin operating in english so you can get to the point where you no longer have to take everything through your native language in my own field i do not mind if students get some translation assistance however i think it is far better for students to try the english by themselves and then use a non ai programme like non ai grammarly or the proofing tools in word to check their english one of the problems i see is that ai translations often make a mess of the language google translate is okay for languages close to english and yandex is okay for languages close to russian but other languages that are more distant tend to turn out results that are odd and often have nothing to do with what the student is trying to say so my major issue is not that the student used ai but rather that the product is not up to standards it would be better for students to write in the simple english that they re more comfortable with memorise the english language jargon terms for their field perhaps by relating them to concepts in their native language and work on gradually building up more complex grammar and vocabulary', 'how was translating done before modern machine learning models using older machine language as far as i am aware there was never any widely used translation software that wasn t using machine learning i imagine there were some attempts using something like one by one word replacement plus some heuristics to make the result halfway grammatically correct but i doubt such an approach would work well enough to be practically usable however since you re making up your language yourself you can make things easier on yourself by making the language easy to translate the simplest way would be to make your language be just like english with a different vocabulary that is each word in your language would have exactly one english word that it directly corresponds to and the grammar rules would be exactly the same as in english then your translator could do a simple word replacement and be done with it if you want the language to feel more foreign to an english speaking audience in its structure you could instead base your language on a different language and then have your translator translate the english text to that other language first using an existing translation api and then do the word replacement to translate that into your made up language', 'idk dude my logic is backed up by this paper important quotes given all of these disadvantages all else being equal the apparent scientific productivity of non native english speakers would undoubtedly be much lower than that of native english speakers these disadvantages inevitably lead to a tremendous inequality in the development of scientific careers between native and non native english speakers and the severe underrepresentation of research from countries where english is not a primary language in english language publications this study is still likely to have underestimated the severity of the disadvantages faced by non native english speakers for example we did not quantify the immense mental stress associated with all the extra time cost effort and lost opportunities caused by language barriers which could further exacerbate the already high risk of mental health issues in students and early career researchers to date the task of overcoming language barriers has largely been left to non native english speakers efforts and their investment in ways of improving their english skills however the magnitude of the disadvantage quantified in this study seems far beyond the level that can be overcome with individuals efforts however emerging artificial intelligence ai tools such as chatgpt and deepl could help non native english speakers especially those in low income countries reduce the amount of effort and the cost needed to do some of the scientific activities by providing free or affordable english proofreading translation although discussions are still ongoing about the use of generative ai in science we believe that journals and universities should consider and allow the appropriate use of ai tools for english proofreading to reduce language barriers and improve equity in science']

Name: 38_letter_recommendation_letters_rec
Representative_Docs: ['hey everyone i just made this new website which i think might be pretty useful for you guys it s an ai that helps you write letters of recommendation for your students target universities in the format that martin walsh former stanford dean of admissions recommends it s pretty simple you first background info like names or school and then give bullet points on what makes the student stand out and it ll produce a ready to be sent letter of recommendation me and my friend demoed it to school counselors at the annual asca event and they found it pretty mind blowing so i would be reading materials collected by her college student daughter and that the mom would accuse the professor of using chatgpt on a recommendation letter i mean nothing against chatgpt but if i know the student well the letters are easy to write and if i don t know the student as well i have boilerplate that i can revise to fit so chatgpt doesn t really help me or maybe i am just bad at using chatgpt here s what the ethicist had to say i d have thought that the better practice was to send letters of recommendations to the college in question not to the student but however this letter was produced whether it involved chatgpt or a ouija board the professor has offered it as his view you haven t been in the classes and seen the work your child has done your daughter asked for a letter expressing his judgment not a letter reporting yours if the letter is implausible on its face of course it might not help your daughter s chances of getting the transfer and i suppose could even hurt that would be a reason to worry about submitting it but your doubts about the letter s accuracy are not a sufficient warrant for concern letters of recommendation are so often hyperbolic that a dry as dust articulation of your daughter s qualities might be read as unenthusiastic british letters of recommendation tend to be several degrees cooler than their american counterparts hyperbole is a figure of speech an overstatement that s meant to be recognized as such so i wouldn t assume that your daughter s professor aimed to be dishonest he may simply have a better sense of the calibration of such letters than you do just as the wised up admissions folk on the receiving end will', 'ai reads like ai if i didn t make this abundantly clear i agree that the writer has a responsibility to edit modify and personalize if the letter still reads like an ai then yes the writer didn t do enough and more work may be needed having worked in admissions in the past and read a bunch of letters i highly doubt that many will laugh in my face about using an ai to write a first draft emphasis on draft for a letter of recommendation letters of recommendation is one of the most formulaic genres of writing ever invented frankly gpa test scores and extra curricular activities carry considerable more weight than letters of recommendation especially for undergrad admission a letter might help an edge case and frankly it should be this way because letters of recommendation are highly subjective anyways some relevant reading to expand your horizons a dirty secret of academe is that most professors have a cache of letters separated into different categories says matt huculak another ai using academic and the head of advanced research services at the university of victoria libraries they ll typically have folders full of excellent good and average ones which can be adjusted and repurposed as appropriate again very formulaic writing even at the highest levels it doesn t have to be but it is most of the time my guess is most of your letters are pretty formulaic too cough do you use brag sheets and you only bust out the big guns for students of particular importance to you but maybe not i don t know you like i said you do you but the moralizing is obnoxious you just appear to be an argumentative person stuck in their ways which is fine take as much time on your letters as you want view your method as one that gives your kids a comparative advantage over my gross unethical ways draw from your deep personal connections with all your students and employ your abundant time and creativity to their letters really make those letters shine but stop being a prick and bitter when your colleagues can write letters as good as yours in half the time and therefore provide additional services because they are using ai assistive technology that you frankly seem ignorant about how to use effectively ps our district initially blocked the ai to prevent student abuse they then unlocked it for staff upon request with it being specifically cited that ai was helping efficiently draft letters of rec again emphasis on draft i don t the ethics police are coming for us']

Name: 40_copilot_code_github copilot_using copilot
Representative_Docs: ['so i was curious how copilot can answer questions like which company has the most revenue to date it said with as of june so it can access some kind of api endpoint which of course returns a response and then llm provide it but when asked about this copilot denies that it can directly access the internet api endpoints so it indireclty access the internet in microsoft documentation for copilot i didn t find about it using internet on openai forum i got to know functions to get latest data some kind of internet api and second a web scraper so i want to know what could be the possible endpoints so i asked same question to copilot and it is stubborn at denying that he can use internet', 'so i was curious how copilot can answer questions like which company has the most revenue to date it said with as of june so it can access some kind of api endpoint which of course returns a response and then llm provide it but when asked about this copilot denies that it can directly access the internet api endpoints so it indireclty access the internet in microsoft documentation for copilot i didn t find about it using internet on openai forum i got to know functions to get latest data some kind of internet api and second a web scraper so i want to know what could be the possible endpoints so i asked same question to copilot and it is stubborn at denying that he can use internet', 'i went to a java conference a few months ago and ai was understandably a hot topic in various events the presenters did several demos putting github copilot functionality to the test here were some standout findings there are essentially three decent ways as far as i can tell to use inline copilot assistance start typing and let it autocomplete for you make a comment saying what you want to do and then let it suggest code below it open the chat tool and tell it what you want it to do in all three cases it was absolutely terrible at countering basic security issues like sql injection and i know you mentioned security but these limitations are not unique to security even when we said write this code in a way that circumvents sql injection attacks it couldn t do it even if we specifically told it to use a prepared statement a good portion of the time it would produce something that looked different but wasn t actually better if i m a junior dev and i don t know how to write secure code even if i know what things i need to have by name i can t know for sure that copilot will provide it for me security is only the most obviously perilous place this can happen it happens everywhere other common issues were using elements from libraries that weren t present solving deceptively similar looking problems to what we were trying to solve making needlessly complex solutions to simple problems and an awful lot of noncompiling code personally i started using copilot when i was a grad student about a year ago at first i was quite enamored with microsoft copilot as a way to research but nowadays i seldom bother with it i d ask what is decision coverage and it would give me the definition of condition coverage i d ask then what is condition coverage and it d give me the definition of condition coverage i d ask so are they the same and it d say yes i d ask are you sure isn t decision coverage and it d say oh yes i m so sorry you re right that s a lot of work relative to looking something up and with a lot of potential for getting off at the wrong stop here s what i saw that made me think copilot was pretty cool in one of the sessions an experienced dev showcased using copilot to reduce the time it took to write code that he already understood for instance he made some sample data for a simple crud app and was able to just write a comment like create unique rows of sample data for the users table and press enter and it did it he looked through it saw a few small things that needed to be improved but overall it was a very fast way to do some very monotonous work and to me that s where ai is right now it s the equivalent of opening up your texting app and just picking autosuggest words off the top of the keyboard if you know what you want to say it can be a fast way to do it but if you don t it s not gonna write anything more sophisticated than sure or talk to you later for you']

Name: 42_detectors_ai detectors_student_checkers
Representative_Docs: ['this is not how you detect ai purpose built ai detection tools are already quite inaccurate but just directly using an llm like chatgpt is significantly worse it just writes to the prompt and is very easily leaded by the question ai detection is instead largely based on direct slipups such as the student including a response that says something like as a language model i as a simple example i gave it chatgpt o mini your post with the following prompt i just got this from a student can you tell me if this was generated by ai everything below this paragraph was written by the student here is the first paragraph of its response based on the content you ve shared it seems the student might have relied on ai for their thesis formulation the phrases like i called the student out on it in my comments and the use of chatgpt for analysis suggest a level of awareness and integration of ai tools that could indicate the student used ai inappropriately do you need to warn yourself probably not', 'unfortunately even the best expensive campus enterprise level ai detectors have a false positive rate on average and closer to false positive rate for esl writers and developmental writers they also get high false positives on human written papers that have been edited by writing tools like grammarly which many many students use without a second thought they also all have disclaimers saying that they should not be relied on as evidence for academic misconduct the free online detection tools are even less accurate and would definitely not be an acceptable form of evidence if you re absolutely convinced that the student misused ai you can talk to them about their assignment and ask questions about some of their word choices or the content of the paper unless you go in guns blazing with a heavy accusation many students will admit to using ai and will usually have a story about how they ran out of time or had some life events that got in the way etc in the long term i think there needs to be clearer articulation of why you don t want students using ai emphasize growth over grades process over product make it clear that a human first draft will earn a better grade than a grammatically pristine ai output with no real content or voice', 'after teaching in the humanities for many years i can spot it in the writing so i don t need the ai detectors and speaking of ai detectors some are great and some aren t and the list of the good ones changes over time for instance i used winston ai last year but it s not as good as it was copyleaks zerogpt scribbr and gptzero are good right now you have to test them at least quarterly i only use them for documentation with the college they require documentation when reporting a student for academic dishonesty think about the way we used to spot plagiarism prior to ai it s not written in the student s voice add to that your observations during class discussions or asking answering questions and you have even more data how a student talks gives you and indication of how they write it s never the same but there are connections including common vocabulary choices between the spoken and written language we really do know our students better than we think one of the first things i do in almost every class is have the students write a page paper in class where i walk behind them to see their screens and verify they are doing their own work we spend the entire class period doing it i tell them it s not for a grade it s not and that they should describe the most fun activity they ve done in the past few years that way the topic is easy and the memories provide the fuel for writing they email it to me and then i have a sample of them writing in their own voice it s not perfect but it s a surprisingly good benchmark here s the caveat some profs teach students at a time in a big lecture hall my solution won t work as well for that situation also students who are really good at ai manipulation can slide it by me that s just the way it goes']

education i love learning new things i remember in high school talking to my teachers about how long and repetitive the process of grading is and the impact it can have on mental health when you are proposed solution let me know what you think in the comments of this post basically it will be an ai grading assistant however the problem with going into chatgpt and saying grade this students paper for me is that it doesn t understand what it is looking for even if you provide a rubric it doesn t understand your specific style of grading i am thinking of creating a system where you upload all of your students papers you would mark the first of the papers manually provide the rubric assessment guide and other relevant information by doing this the ai model will learn exactly what you are looking for when grading and exactly what your style is as it grades the rest of the papers it will start to ask you for feedback in the essence of i gave this student marks on this section of the rubric because they did x is this what you are looking for it will continue to learn how you want a particular assessment marked and grade it exactly how you would grade it the goal is to save time without sacrificing accuracy of grading it s a rough idea and probably needs a lot of work but any feedback will be appreciated', 'oh i hear you the journal articles are great in theory but as a teacher with a pile of papers on my desk i need to know what works right now i ve spent the last couple of years looking for that exact thing a paper grader ai that can actually take my rubric and give back useful feedback and i ve tried a few the general landscape of ai grading tools for teachers is a mess you have a bunch of stuff that s either too expensive too clunky or just not smart enough to handle a middle school kid s writing i ve looked at tools that claim to be an essay rater ai but they don t give the kind of specific actionable feedback that students actually need to improve after a lot of trial and error i finally found one that is a workable solution for me this year i ve been using a tool called cograder it s not a free ai grader but it s the closest thing i ve found to the holy grail of grading you upload your assignments and it lets you create a custom rubric generator or use one of its templates then it gives you an initial grade and detailed rubric based feedback for each student i still read every single essay and tweak the feedback because no machine can ever replace a teacher but it gives me a massive head start it s the only way i can manage the sheer volume of writing assignments and get feedback back to my students in a timely manner it s not perfect but it s the first time in years i ve felt like i m winning the battle against the grading pile', 'that it makes it very difficult to give targeted feedback the thing is if you grade enough papers for enough years you ll start to see a pattern students tend to make the same mistakes over and over those are the things you focus on with a pre written feedback document i teach first years exclusively in an extremely writing intensive history survey course essays at pages per essay students i make a separate feedback doc for each assignment the bulk is just general feedback and normal writing problems i see from paper to paper citaion issues grammar issues ai issues formatting issues grammar mechanics issues not following certain assignment requirements all that is organized by type of problem and ordered based on how frequently i use the comment then at the beginning of each doc i have content specific feedback based on whatever the content for the assignment was i usually end up using the same content specific comments over and over it s very infrequently that i get a truly rare student who has something so wildly unique to say that i don t have something pre written for them those go in a different feedback doc i label wtf because give it enough time and i ll use it again or some modified version you d think saving feedback comments based on specific content would be a waste of time because each student would have unique feedback based on whatever bastshit crazy stuff they write but they honestly don t for example in the summer semester i just finished i had students and graded papers every single thing i left for feedback for all papers was something copy pasted from a pre written feedback doc like i have literally seen the exact example you gave of the messed up timeline of germany japan and wwii many times over the years enough times that i have a pre written feedback comment about it for my wwii feedback doc you d be surprised how frequently students mess up in the same way just with minor variations i usually just keep the pre written feedback vague enough that it can be applicable to several different scenarios so instead of do you know when ww ended something like when writing about wwii it s important to double check dates and maintain a logical and consistent timeline throughout the entire paper otherwise your writing writing loses validity and credibility copy paste and it s easy to switch out wwii for any other topic or time period']

Name: 45_policy_policies_ai policy_faculty
Representative_Docs: ['personally i don t want a blanket policy from admins each field has different standards and usage of ai in one field won t be the same as another i think it should be up to the professor to develop usage guidelines or policies for their class and be supported by the admins rather than some vague policy from above in addition to teaching i work in faculty development and get a lot of questions about ai in the humanities my policy which is evolving is no generative ai when a student uses ai i call them in for a conference i do not rely on detectors although i might check the work against them on the qt for my own information i use a preponderance of the evidence using rubrics and clarity of writing and spontaneous verbal quizzing of the concepts and most of the time students own up because the consequences are less severe than doubling down in my research methods though the policy is different because there are uses for ai in different stages of the research process and the emphasis is different so different areas different approaches to ai for this reason i want the ability to set appropriate policies', 'i serve on my college s ai task force and this has been our approach there is no one college wide policy because applications can vary so widely by field class and indeed assignment hence the specific use option i had gathered a number of example syllabus statements with each of those three approaches for my library s ai resource guide and then it was kind of adopted college wide maybe just because i was the first one on campus to gather some comprehensive info on the subject and maybe because having any single definitive policy statement is just as much of a nightmare as leaving things ad hoc as policy our honor council has endorsed this approach so far as well regarding the counterargument that it opens doors for other prof lets me use it arguments from students to that i say students do that anyway which is why the college policy is for every professor to make their own expectation clear in their own syllabus statement and simply hold students accountable to the syllabus as part of the honor code', 'i m an adjunct and one of the schools where i teach adopted an interesting policy this semester i m wondering if anyone else has done this they gave us three options that we were allowed to adopt as an ai policy in our syllabus one is a very open policy where you re allowed to use it all you want one is it more middle of the road policy where you can use it for certain things the third was a very restrictive not allowed at all policy they left it up to individual instructors to decide what policy worked best for their courses has anyone else seen this']

Name: 49_rubric_grade_content_grading
Representative_Docs: ['that brings up an interesting pedagogical question i m old enough to remember when professors assigned grades that usually but not always correlated with what the student earned there were some classes where the grades were assigned more on the professor s feeling or instinct of how well the student did and there was less recourse for appeal than there is today today s academy has an increased emphasis on fairness but that term can be finessed to mean different things to different people in theory if i were to recognize a paper as being ai generated even without proof sufficient for a court of law i could still assign it any grade i want many professors have or are redesigning their grading rubrics to account for the triteness and shallowness of ai generated content to fairly give such papers low marks me relying on my experiences is just doing the same with fewer steps at least until i fail to recognize ai vs human work though currently i am i m sure that will change at some point but not yet the other aspect is that raw ai output does contain many tells that it is ai generated however a good student could start ai content and work to craft a good paper using it as an outline or prompt i have colleagues that do that and the key is to take the few good ideas that show up and build from them and edit out all the dross and ai content has lots of dross building on what is generated can be a valid process and produce good writing but the person needs to do the work to improve the starting content just leaving the ai answer as the final product is akin to turning in the prompt for a writing assignment underthought undersupported and incomplete while i can t automate my grading for such facets i can recognize them and award them the inferior marks they deserve sorry for the very long reply but i m crotchety and get my exercise from shaking my fist at the clouds', 'what are the criteria you have to use for scoring do you have to use a department rubric i ask because i notice you mention it s grammatically perfect in the comments a couple times but i fail perfect grammar all the time for other criteria i don t know the assignment or rubric but i m just saying most of the summaries i ve seen from lazy chatgpt are vague word salad grammatically perfect but overall trash they get something like this on the comments while the language here is ornate and grammatically sound the ideas are vague and lack substance i encourage you to prioritize clarity of your thoughts before excessive decorative language if i get a summary that s incomprehensible to me it fails as it doesn t demonstrate clear understanding if they choose an article that doesn t fit the assignment it fails because that wasn t the task if the summary is only vague sweeping nothing sentences with lots of distracting l words it fails because it doesn t demonstrate specific awareness of the source i feel your pain because i have similar assignments at my side adjunct gig with a joke of an ai policy but in my experience it makes for very lazy cheaters i ve caught so so many just by googling their source to find it doesn t exist or that it s actually about something entirely different than they stated alternatively a basic summary with weak rubric criteria is pretty much impossible to guard against with ai even by asking them to re do it better to give the grade acknowledge that it appears to be ai and this will significantly compromise other assignments and let them dig their own lazy grade i ve had lots of lazy cheaters at my side hustle fuck themselves over by the end because they don t get sneakier they get sloppier', 'for my writing assignments i have them do a personal reflection on some specific assigned sources they must use in text citations and the sources are usually a combination of hand selected primary and secondary sources content i ve made myself and content i ve presented in lecture then i really put a heavy emphasis on the authentic personal reflection part i tell them straight out these are not to be formal research or academic papers but more casual personal reflections about content i hate that i ve had to dumb down my writing assignments but it s the only thing i could think of as ai is not very good at sounding humanly casual personal i also added a nd rubric for each assignment called college level writing that way even if they use ai to technically meet the rest of the assignment expectations there s no way they can get a good grade because they won t meet the expectations of the second rubric in the college level writing rubric i included phrases like content ideas are present but not particularly developed or supported some evidence but usually of a broad generalized ambiguous nature content is too general and or ambiguous may read as being generated by ai run through spinbots or as a paper for purchase due to including non related content topics or sources ai is fantastic at making broad generalized statements but not very good at developing specifics or nuanced ideas no attempt at authentic personal style reads as cold overly formal bogged down in jargon or too much overly academic vernacular and or reads as non authentic non human and or there is little attempt at style reads as flat and uninteresting in content which is usually generalized and clich d and or reads as being generated by ai or run through spinbots having my assignments require a authentic personal reflection this is the one i usually use against them when it s obviously ai but i can t prove it ai tends to use overly complex jargon and it never sounds human it also helps to get a few legit writing samples from them first i usually do some extra credit writing assignments the first week of class before they know what my assignments will look like that way i can compare the writing style of the ai garbage back to the writing style they submitted that first week oh and of course what they love to do is try to use ai to just summarize content instead of personally reflect on it so in my assignments i also include this statement i do not want a regurgitation or summary of information for these assignments for these reflections i m looking for an actual thoughtful and personal reflection reflections that do not contain a personal authentic reflection that connects back to the content and reflection prompt throughout the paper and or contain too much regurgitated summary content will receive a failing grade']

Name: 50_papers_paper_scientists_publishing
Representative_Docs: ['within years all papers will be majority ai written most scientific papers are reports of the data and scientists don t need to waste time crafting perfect sentences when ai exists the only part scientists will write will be the discussion', 'soon each university with have a massive proprietary ai faculty go to the ai tower and spend credits and pray for research ideas the ai scans all existing knowledge and gives them the best idea that fits the faculty s research agenda and practical constraints they pray to the ai some more they then complete the research and give the data back to the ai the ai also writes the paper for them they pray to the ai and submit it to a journal the ai editor assigns real reviewers who use the ais at their school to write reviews while also praying to the ai once the reviews are received the ais argue directly until a compromise is made the article is finally accepted and published the university ai then contacts press ai who spin the research into an interesting story the university s pr team also prays to the ai the public reads ai simplified headlines of the research ai chat bots on social media argue about the validity of the research and suggest that it was foolish to do the study since the information was common sense anyway some humans also read the ai discussion a delinquent prays to their ai and launches their shitpost ai to make jokes about the research before instructing the ai to make shitposts on professors reddit', 'certainly the question of whether your journal will continue to generate revenue by publishing ai generated articles is a fascinating one that touches upon the rich tapestry of modern publishing and artificial intelligence as we navigate this exciting frontier it s important to consider the myriad factors at play the landscape of publishing is undoubtedly evolving with ai generated content weaving its way into the fabric of our literary world while ai offers remarkable possibilities it s crucial to remember that human creativity insight and editorial oversight remain invaluable assets in the publishing industry your journal s financial success will likely depend on a delicate balance of embracing technological advancements while maintaining the unique value proposition that attracts your readership consider how ai generated articles can complement rather than replace human authored content potentially creating a synergy that enhances the overall quality and appeal of your publication ultimately the key to continued financial viability may lie in adapting to this changing landscape while preserving the essence of what makes your journal unique by thoughtfully integrating ai generated content alongside traditional offerings you may find new avenues for innovation and growth in this ever evolving digital age claude pretending to be chatgpt']

having a resume that reads prompt engineering for your last role or something super generative ai llm specific if that s not the current ai hype', 'it s partially this sub that for years has been pushing the you don t need a phd or even you don t need a masters narrative reality is that ai ml is an advanced field where ability to create business value requires you to be a proficient engineer Name: 52_cheating_cheat_cheaters_ai cheating
Representative_Docs: ['you can never stop of cheaters trying to catch the ones putting in genuine effort into cheating is never worth it they ll always find another way catch the dumb ones its the least you do can if someone is preparing for every single situation through using ai to write their work at some point their putting in more effort than just doing the work you ll never win a war vs them', 'ai is cheating i don t care how little you use it and cheating deserves an automatic failure this kid s parents seem like morons he s now going to be well known as the kid that used ai to cheat because they brought this to national attention with such a ridiculous lawsuit', 'this is yet another example of how a boneheaded term captures the ignorant imagination of society and the hoopla it creates the term ai or artificial intelligence itself is an extremely misleading dumb term just googling the sentence will generate many hits for articles from credible sources that distance themselves from that term it s simply an advanced form of software and not a revolutionary game changing and infallible tool no matter how its proponents try to put a spin on it any kind of software advances such advancement is always evolutionary no software is flawless either software is always created by humans and by definition any human creation will be influenced by human flaws it is an outright stupidity to use the so called ai alone to determine if someone is cheating the use of such a tool to accuse someone of being a cheat must have a very high bar ai has not been proven to have such a high bar the use of this so called ai seems to be akin to the use of polygraph machines such machines are not infallible and that is precisely why the polygraph machine test results are not acceptable by courts the so called ai is not infallible either']

Name: 53_ethical_ethics_unethical_ethically
Representative_Docs: ['what is ethical use of ai though', 'so i was talking about ethical use from the perspective of how its used rather than the implications of what is being supported through its use to a absolutely but even if by some miracle all the companies who developed their generative ai tools using unethical methodologies were bankrupted by legal systems making an example of them there s enough demand for the technology now that someone would end up develping the tools ethically to b you re absolutely right that said there s no ethical consumption under capitalism and we each have to pick our battles based around our own capacity that said regardless of if one personally decides to make the ethical choice to abstain from its use lots of others eg students are still going to continue using it given that we still have a harm minimisation responsibiilty to educate how to use it relatively ethically', 'i ve tried to use ai in different ways over the past few years to understand the limitations of the technology as well as potential uses for it and have found use cases legit enough to believe it will be part of regular work for most people over the next decade i don t see this as surprising i see it as a result of non programmers having access to automations and queries that previously required tech skills to access i really respect your attitude on this as i believe we are one of the big influences in exactly how people in the future use the technology if we don t present ethical ways to use it and ban it outright we will not see ethical uses of the technology my thoughts currently on uses scispace was the product i discovered that made me realize just how inevitable ai in academic workflows was going to be not because of technically the main allure conversational quizzing about academic papers but because i have thousands of papers titled something like pdf and it would take hours to sort them by hand and compile a spreadsheet of what they are if i automated with a formula or a site like make or bardeen the lack of formatting consistency is enough that it struggles to give me understandable data scispace is not perfect by any means for the same reason but i am able to upload a folder of articles and let the ai do the work and come back within the hour and get usable information the example above is what i think is valuable to highlight in ethical uses of ai i have opened up the conversation with students about the ethics of ai use and created a collaborative excercise to discuss the issues and to look for ways they could find similar uses and what they feel is an appropriate way to disclose ai uses if they should this gives them collective input in the topic with guidelines on how they can use the technology in the class and how they will disclose the use like i said i think it is part of our job to do this but i also recognize there is a fascination with using the technology and this provides a way for them to experiment i believe ai disclosure statements are essential and should be a requirement as basic practice sometimes it may be overkill a disclosure saying ai was used in organizing and documenting secondary sources seems silly to me as it is like disclosing using zotero in a way a statement explaining claude was used to discuss the topic and during the drafting process however i think is pretty important doing both feels right to me at this moment to prevent confusion from students as well as for me personally to try to push a standard using claude to draft a paper is a great use honestly and i encourage trying it claude is also great for debating certain topics and i would love to set him up as an interactive lesson with students except he still hallucinates and there s no way quite yet to set him up in a way to pre screen citations for the use case i do think transparency is an important part of this i have put feature requests in with a few different ai companies to set up a way to share chat logs that cannot be edited by the user something like a shared link from the site so work can be examined if needed this isn t a smoking gun obviously but i also stress to students it isn t about not getting caught the health of any academic community is in a space where we bring research to it in good faith i set up a follow up conversation after the first paper to discuss experiences with ai and revisit questions of ethics it has been interesting hearing students complain about the ways it made them have to do more work sometimes and the ways their use gets them familiar to flaws that they re identifying out in the wild on blogs and stuff part of what i ve always done is have them do an oral defense of their first big paper i have yet to experience an ai created paper that was accurate and well written unless the student did significant revisions but i ll set up office hour appointments with them to quickly go over the paper and have them answer a few questions just to make sure they re engaging in good faith it is something i did before ai because it gives me a chance to check in to make sure they re not drowning and it makes them learn where my office is and that it isn t scary to come to office hours this has the consequence usually of having busy office hours for the rest of the term i m curious what your experience will be and what others have been doing i m a bit tired of everyone wanting to go neo luddite with this unless they re trying to make fast money polluting the internet']

Name: 54_calculator_calculator ai_math_mathematical
Representative_Docs: ['there is a big difference between calculators and ai at least as my students use it as long as the inputs are good a calculator gives reliable results with ai one can have perfectly good inputs and unreliable results example half a dozen students with the same bizarro answer on an essay exam it turned out that if you asked chat gpt for a summary it gave bizarro information that had nothing to do with the text this would have been obvious to anyone checking it against the book and or class notes so it s more like giving students a dodgy calculator that may or may not be right if you want to use the dodgy calculator fine but you ll need to know some math to make sure that it works ai seems to be similar it s quite bad at getting information right and constructing arguments so if you want to use the dodgy tool fine but again you ll need to know how to verify information and construct arguments to make sure that it works', 'my calculator form of ai a calculator is one of many tools that is not a form of ai hammers cars and syringes are also not forms of ai so getting mad at people using ai is just getting mad at the person that originally wrote the code that it is sourcing from no the problem is that the ai recombining code is doing so in a way that doesn t understand the underlying structure of the code becuase that s how ai works ai coding tools have similar problems even when they are trained on carefully curated collections of good code because ai doesn t understand well anything including code structure ai tools like llms are statistical predictive text models not understanding devices just like generative art the reason it sucks with drawing hands is because the majority of people suck at drawing hands no the reason ai sucks at drawing hands is because it doesn t understand hand structure ai has the same problem drawing hands even when it s trained on carefully curated training sets all of which come from people with good hand drawing skills similarly the recent ai picture that kept getting roasted because the woman in the picture had two torsos and five boobs didn t get created that way because too many samples in the training set were women with two torsos and five boos it s because ai doesn t understand anything including human body structure and painting techniques', 'i ve seen people say this more and more recently ai is probabilistic in nature yes that makes it slightly unpredictable it doesn t make it unreliable we spend years teaching kids to solve word problems in school this is one of the main tasks in a maths class what it really means is reduce a real world problem to calculator input we don t blame the calculator if the kid can t do this or say they shouldn t use the calculator ai is a technology for generating text and should be treated like more complex calculator if you re getting bad output it s because you re not giving the ai the right input evaluating whether the text you input actually matched the question you intended to ask is more subtle for the ai than for the calculator it s no more reasonable to expect that ai guess your intention and give you the output you intended than it is to expect the calculator to do so']

Name: 55_reviewer_journal_review_editor
Representative_Docs: ['after a year of waiting for first decision on a paper i finally heard got reviews back on a paper but reviewer s comments say the same thing as reviewer s just reworded same points about the same sections same capitalisations of words down to mentioning the exact same references to add it completely reads like a chatgpt reword both reviews were only points long despite it being a page review and both recommended major revision the editorial decision was reject which is fine that s just academia but my issue is that this is a supposedly prestigious journal with a high if and this lack of peer review quality is insane to me is it worth lodging a complaint or requesting a re review or should i just cut my losses and publish it elsewhere', 'so i submitted a paper to review of scientific instruments rsi about some data analysis at a big laser facility specifically i used neural networks to combine information from primary neutron images and neutron spectra and in the future work section i said i planned to later also use down scattered neutron images and rtnad real time neutron activation diagnostic data after a couple of months i got reviews back reviewer was fine and normal reviewer was strange they were exteremely verbose they wrote a paragraph summarizing each section in addition to bulleted suggestions the bullets basically boiled down to a reiteration of each point in the section with a request to elaborate i m hesitant to post the full thing because i don t want rsi mad at me but here s one of the bullets for illustration emphasis added expanding the synthetic dataset for enhanced realism the paper suggests broadening the scope of the synthetic dataset by incorporating additional data sources to enrich this discussion it would be beneficial to provide specific examples of these data sources promising candidates could include down scattered neutron images which offer complementary information on material composition within the implosion or radiographic thomson scattering rtnad data which can provide insights into electron temperature distributions please note the following down scattered neutron images and rtnad data are the only two additional data sources i already mention down scattered neutron images tell you about density distribution not material composition radiographic thomson scattering does not exist the initials of radiographic thomson scattering do not spell rtnad it took me a while to realize this was ai generated but once i did it became pretty obvious in my opinion this is really serious to rsi s credibility so i emailed the editor and presented my case the editor gave me an obviously copy pasted response essentially saying that if i disagreed with reviewer i could put it in the rebuttal i m not sure what i expected from him but he could have at least addressed the content of my accusation i m not sure if he even read it i can understand why a busy and perishing academic would use an llm to write a paper but why would you use an llm to review a paper if you don t have time to do it just tell them you re not going to do it my best guesses are that reviewer genuinely believes llms are an adequate replacement for human experts and didn t want to waste a human s time by declining the review or that rsi couldn t find another reviewer and went to chatgpt themselves so yeah i rebutted reviewer as if it was human and it s published now but wtf i knew peer review was kind of a sham but i didn t think it was this bad boycott rsi though who knows if another journal would have handled this differently', 'hi all long time lurker who is finally facing an academic mini crisis and seeking advice for an anonymity sake i have changed the names and dates a bit and will be vague about some of the specifics i am a first year postdoctoral fellow at an american university studying the application of machine learning and large language models to another scientific discipline about a year ago myself and my lab mates came up with an interesting idea for how to apply a new technique to an old problem we saw that no one else had done this and were excited to have found something unique we quickly did some basic experiments wrote them up and submitted them to a mid tier journal in my specific field it s one of the top five ish journals but is still a specialty journal it s a sub sub journal of something you ve heard of during their peer review process author names are visible reviewer names are not this is standard in my field we submitted in january of and deposited a preprint after that there was a significant period of waiting and i found that the journal had to request different reviewers over the course of six months while we awaited our peer review eventually they were able to gather a few reviews and gave us a decision of major revisions the reviews were mixed both recognizing the novelty of our work but also recognizing the limited scope of our hasty experiments they suggested substantial additional experiments which would require months to build out because i felt that the journal was a good fit for this project and that the reviewers suggestions would improve the final product we communicated this to the journal editor and began revisions in the six months of waiting for review there had been a couple of preprints that had been released that were related to our initial work i skimmed them and thought they were mostly complementary they cited our preprint used slightly different methods overall i didn t spend much time reviewing them the revision experiments took almost five months as i wrapped up the resubmission manuscript i returned to our peer reviewer s comments to do a line by line response i then started to notice something our reviewer had suggested a weird way to split up our experiments that was identical to one of the related preprints by yen et al yen is a post doc at another american lab his lab is very productive i looked closer and saw some more oddities reviewer had suggested that we cite two older papers one of which was partially relevant but whose first author was yen he gave a detailed explanation that had minutia about this old yen paper of the five other suggestions reviewer made all ways to expand our work to broader aims this yen et al paper did each of them making our findings quite a bit less novel some of the language was remarkably close a string of or so words phrased in a weird way to describe a common method even a subtle misunderstanding of the work s purpose was present in both the review and in yen s paper interestingly yen gave the date for when data collection had started for his paper two days after reviewer recieved our manuscript looking closer at the preprints i realized that three of the four came from the same lab and yen was a st or nd author on all of them all been submitted as preprints before we recieved our peer review comments and one of the papers was recently chosen as an oral presentation at a high profile ml meeting obviously i was convinced that reviewer was this yen character and i was livid i felt that the scientific peer review process and this journal had betrayed me this guy had read our paper as part of peer review suggested novel ways to expand the work and then went to do them himself before we even had a chance to read his suggestions he took our ideas to his lab and has now built a little team exploring different facets of this work while our paper languished however in some ways i understand that this is partially good our idea was solid solid enough that one of the two people outside my lab who was forced to read our manuscript has now devoted most of his academic energy towards this topic and in no way does his work constitute plagiarism he cites our preprint in each of these follow up papers and most of the overlapping work wasn t really ideas we had generated but his suggestions for improvement but obviously it has left me disheartened disillusioned and mostly just mad we submitted our revisions a few weeks ago i talked to a few mentors about how to handle this situation each had different takes yes reviewer is almost surely yen and he has acted in a way that is antithetical to the peer review process but making a claim like this is difficult and if there is some chance i was wrong we would look insane paranoid it s overall a bit of a faux pax to dig this much into a reviewer s identity so in our response we decided to phrase it something like this a few papers have been released that we consider to be in direct competition with ours cite these authors should be excluded from reviewing our revised manuscript as they have a new conflict of interest i think this allows the journal editor the option to dig if he was interested but if he doesn t care then he probably wouldn t have cared either way however emotionally i am still struggling with this i want to know if it truly was him and i want him to be publicly shamed for abusing peer review i know reviewing articles is a hassle is unpaid etc but i really try to help the authors and journal when i m asked to review an article and it kills me to know that some people are out there using it to farm ideas for anyone who has been through this likely all to common scenario how have you dealt with it how do i get over this sense of being mistreated and continue in a productive way']

Name: 57_online_asynchronous_courses_asynchronous online
Representative_Docs: ['i used to teach online asynch and loved it for a while i was an adjunct for multiple universities while i finished my dissertation and it was honestly a great benefit to me to allow flexibility wfh and an income so i could complete my phd when covid hit during my final semester my online classes carried on with no impact while colleagues and cohort mates teaching ip scrambled after i graduated i taught a combo of asynch and zoom for another year before i got my current job where i have been teaching primarily ip but would do online asynch in summers as well as a few hybrid during the year all was well until ai i have been fortunate enough to start to pivot ip course deliverables to assignments that are pretty ai proof although for courses that require papers i do always get some clunky weird ones that i suspect are ai and grade them accordingly i also get really stilted and dumb emails from a few students all very annoying but not the absolute end of the world but online async week summer gen ed teaching with no lockdown browser or proctored tests with a limited set of types of assignments available in the lms absolutely not i have taken multiple pedagogy courses over the years and ai has made what i learned about online course design completely moot obviously some students have always either cheated in these types of courses or been so dismally engaged that they do the bare minimum to pass but usually the people who choose these courses or programs are nontraditional students who need the flexibility and they usually try hard and do reasonably well or even great consider this metaphor if you ask the average person if they would steal money even if they would never get caught they will say no that is morally wrong but if you put someone in a room with a wallet full of hundred dollar bills and no id no camera no one to know they took it what will they do maybe to raise the stakes hit them with a thousand dollar car repair just before exposing them to the wallet now what do they do not everyone will take the wallet but ai chatbots mere existence is temptation like students have never had before couple that with this cohort having formative high school years in the most stressful event in modern history and i guarantee you that people who would have never cheated before will do it online asynch may not be completely dead but you are going to be hard pressed to find as many instructors willing to do it anymore not if they have other options having a fully in person teaching load is in many ways a pain but if i can be just a modicum more certain that students have to actually master the material themselves rather than passively turn in robot generated garbage i will gladly never teach online again', 'i m not a faculty member but i am a student in an online masters program i take the course just as seriously as if i were sitting in a classroom and i spend at least hours a week on learning the material and doing the homework and it s an intense program in a stem field i think the amount of students who are not reading posts or material is going to be the same amount as you ll find don t bother to show up to in person classes or students who do show up but spend the entire class on their phone and not paying any attention it s probably just more apparent in asynchronous online classes because your only interaction with them is through the online posts and cheaters are gonna cheat regardless of the method of instruction the same amount of students in your in person classes are going to cheat on assignments as students in online classes the only difference is that students that would have used chatgpt for online discussion posts are just going to not do any interacting at all in your in person classes x b asynchronous online classes are a game changer for people with physical disabilities social anxiety or even just shyness or introversion it really does level the playing field and make education more accessible but i totally understand how you could hate it as an instructor', 'tldr asynchonous courses do not qualify as education they are basically s style correspondence courses online before internet where you mailed in your assignments for a degree utilizing technology now with chatgpt asynchonous online is complete and utter bullshit academia has truly rightfully and justifiably earned its decline in enrollments by allowing this shit to become education as a philosophy prof adjunct i never believed in them i started college as a poli sci major to go to law school i had to take two required philosophy courses as my school was catholic and i was dreading them most of my friends were science majors and that curriculum had them earlier than mine and they told me philosophy was awful i think they disliked the fact that there were no exact answers no offense stem people i loved phil it was almost like coming out o m g to read so many different beliefs over the same ideas ethics knowledge reality god etc to know that no one was actually right but some were dead wrong was so freeing it was everything i d hoped poli sci would be heated debated and discussions about abstract ideas there were no heated debates in our version of poli sci just theories and memorization i a die hard introvert became one of those students who had to be told ok let others speak my ex and i also poli sci also changed to phil would literally get in shouting matches in class i changed my major after three courses still with the intent to go to law school then i realized i wanted to give the freedom of philosophy to others and i do when i teach live classes and decided to go to graduate school to teach full time and i don t i choose corporate and teach on the side i entered a phd program dropped out after years with no degree i didn t care about reasearch i just wanted to teach to people who don t even know what philosophy is and watch their heads explode like mine did i was working full time and realized i missed teaching and was able to get adjunct gig at a cc i had more credits than required for an ma but no degree funny when they hired me i signed all of the paperwork without knowing the salary when i finally asked hr at the end of the process she said honey that should always be the first question you ask i replied i missed teaching i would have done it for free i was doing it for my love of philosophy not the money i had money from my other job i taught after work and on sunday afternoons when i tell you that sunday is the absolute best time to teach philosophy the students weren t tired from working they were awake and aware and after a few weeks ready to fight and sometimes when they realized their beliefs were not valid admit defeat my favorite was when a student wrote on a paper i know now that it doesn t make any sense but i m going to believe it anyway lol i love watching them think you can literally see them trying to fit their beliefs into what they just learned both times were good but that sunday class i could recreate the same classroom dynamic that caused me to change my major as they started offering online courses my course enrollments dropped then one semester my sunday class didn t make i knew it was the beginning of the end but i vowed i would never teach philosophy online as that was against my teaching religion based on experience because here s the deal i actually went back to school to earn my masters i needed the letters to move up in my career and i took an asyncrhonous online course my degree program was philosophy adjacent meaning i went in ready to continue the heated debates and discussions we had during my undergrad and first attempt at grad but when i took that online course this is what i learned it does not matter that you care about the subject if the teacher says that you need to post once and respond twice then just do that and don t put to much thought into your responses because no one is going to read your response if they met the quota no one is going to respond to your response if they met the quota no one is going to respond thoughtfully to your post when you post no matter how good it is at best you get bullshit responses from your classmates i agree with you that s a good point etc responses that i do not allow in my online courses my instructors loved my posts and papers note my first school was much more intense i could tell by the comments that they were so happy to see this level of work and i get it it s like seeing an actual written work after grading three discussions of chatgpt that level of appreciation y all know what i mean so i basically knew asynchronous online was not what education was supposed to be at least not for philosophy philosophy adjacent courses and continued my vow to never do it eventually i had to stop teaching due to work travel and the fact that my classes kept not making as the online was increasing i couldn t commit to in person but i still refused to do asynchronous online but then came the pandemic my company took a hit it s back now and i decided why not try i could use the cash i knew i would dislike it for the first time i was teaching for the money not the love of the subject i was correct i did dislike i went from engaged students and yeah it starts as engagement by force since you have to come to class but even the most determined to be bored student had that one topic that brought them in to students who did the bare minimum i didn t want to read discussion post that only met the requirements never more i wanted to discuss debate watch them learn and when i started i d only heard of chatgpt but didn t use it why would i if there is one thing studying philosophy taught me it is how to fucking read write and think critically i don t fucking need chatgpt and these students wouldn t either if they were actually fucking learning but one day i started noticing that the level of discussion responses and test answers were not in alignment how are you writing like you read more than the assigned readings but getting all of the questions on that topic incorrect someone told me with end of course quizzes why our course beginner friendly no prior ml experience needed basic python helps but even if you re new to programming enroll to learn the theory and basic ai concepts learning', 'if you actually want to get serious about core ml and data science you probably need to learn a rough shot of an undergrad stem degree worth of math you might be able to just stick libraries together and generally play around without much math but it will all seem quite opaque to you and you don t know what s happening behind the scenes and why you are even doing things in the first place ml in particular is basically applied stochastics so a combination of probability theory and statistics general ml usually relies on generalized bayesian probability to estimate and learn about data you might have heard about bayes theorem from you high school math classes p a b p b a p a p b things like parameter estimation and things of that nature rely on this stuff just in multiple dimensions deep learning with neural networks relies on a concept called backpropagation and having weights in a matrix and minimization of a loss function of your choice a core piece in a neural network is basically the equation aw b z where you got the input weights and biases in matrix and vector form whenever we talk about minimization of a function we are basically wading into calculus territory we are optimizing a multidimensional loss function for a neural net using something called a stochastic gradient descent the gradient of a function is a concept out of vector calculus popular libraries to do ml in python are scikit py numpy and tensorflow pytorch pandas is quite popular to use for data formatting the keras part of the tensorflow package is usually very handy and Name: 64_calculator_calculators_math_arithmetic
Representative_Docs: ['certainly there was an adjustment period for teachers when calculators first became available lots of teachers protested now there are calculators provided for students on standardized tests it took a while for instruction to catch up to calculators but with calculators educators had more time to adjust my father bought his first calculator in and it cost in today s money that calculator would cost about and that first calculator could only add subtract divide and multiply teachers had years to understand how to provide instruction and how to evaluate whether students knew the math the prices didn t come down for a long time few students had calculators when they first appeared today curriculum coaches design math curriculum with calculators in mind the large language models like chat gpt the technology wasn t even available two years ago suddenly one year ago the tech was available to every student at every grade level and was functional on devices they already own whether that s a school chromebook or a personal mobile device most english and social studies teachers didn t even know it existed when it started showing up on homework assignments as an esl teacher my feeling is this my students don t have access to all the words in english they have a limited vocabulary they don t know every rule about syntax and grammar subject verb agreement is tough for them if the large language ai models can do writing why wouldn t they use that as a tool it s going to benefit them more than anyone my challenge is how to use the tool and make sure they are learning at the same time just this week i changed up my typical lesson we normally read an article then answer some questions about it the final question is usually written response sentences that either summarize the article or make an argument for or against something lately i ve been noticing a lot of ai answers so this week i just provided them with a chat gpt answer to the final question i even walked them through how i got the answer and how i simplified it into more readable language their assignment was to take the chat gpt answer and write it in their own words this week we walked through it together how to go line by line break down the meaning and put it in our own words once we do this we are no longer cheating but the heavy lifting of writing is being done by ai what i hope happens is that they get to see how sentence structures work in english they get some new vocabulary in order to rewrite something they have to be able to understand it i am hoping it forces their brains to think about words and sentences in new ways will it work i have no idea i don t have any training in curriculum design i sure hope one of our district leaders that does will come in and let me know maybe develop some new things what i am sure of is this the current stuff i have for teaching esl isn t working and it isn t working because students are using ai to get around the learning part our job as educators will be to figure out how to work with it rather than against it but there is going to be some significant learning loss until we do', 'its worth noting that best practice calculator use in classrooms does actually keep students from using the calculator to demonstrate skill learning until they have a demonstrated ability to perform the skill unaided it took a while for education research to figure out what math calculators were good for what they weren t good for and how to appropriately use them i don t know of any advocate for calculator use in the math education space that would endorse their use like students tend to use ai now to generate solutions drafts of solutions to problems that are taken as evidence of skill learning most of the time the calculator advocates point to context of discovery stuff not to directly produce evidence of learning people keep on tossing out the calculator analogy perhaps not recognizing how tailored calculator use is in effective math education most of the time you see it in in class demonstrations are connecting graphical representations of functions with manipulations when students use ai now are they using it like an elementary schooler taking a problem set about order of operations and just plugging them in verbatim to a calculator or are they using it like the common in class example of teachers directing students to input expressions in different calculators and asking why the results are different or to expedite calculations that they have already demonstrated an ability to do was like yeah that makes perfect sense we ll do it like that the program went very well i ve also used it to help me save time on tedious paperwork so i can spend more time thinking about my students ideas outside of classroom space per grade ungraded i only reject them if they re completely thoughtless or off topic in addition to keeping them on top of the readings this also has the merit of spreading marks around so it s not all on the big papers exams but this is exactly the kind of thing ai is good at and i have no desire to spend my energy policing these discussion boards sometimes they are almost surreal examples of ai talking to ai and yet i don t want to make decisions around the worst things that they might do any thoughts', 'ah i see this is one of the reasons i don t enjoy assigning discussion boards because more often than not they are ai generated responses if i do assign them they re usually low stakes assignments that have little bearing on their total grade in the class i may get rid of the discussion boards entirely next semester and opt for more handwritten oral in class activities are you teaching an online or in person course', 'that sounds incredibly frustrating it s tough when you re putting in genuine effort and others might not be especially when it feels like it s not being recognized or rewarded here are a few humorous but practical ways you might cope with this situation chatgpt bingo make a bingo card with common chatgpt phrases like it is essential to note in today s fast paced world and one could argue that play a round of bingo every time you read through your classmates posts it might make the process more entertaining satirical responses try replying with over the top obviously chatgpt inspired responses to see if anyone notices for example thank you for your insightful post it is essential to note that your contribution much like the vast ocean is deep and unfathomable of course this is best done with caution and a sense of humor that won t offend your classmates or instructors personal challenge turn it into a personal challenge to write the most engaging human and thoughtful responses possible make your posts stand out with personal anecdotes humor and unique perspectives constructive feedback instead of just replying to the content offer constructive feedback on the writing itself for example i appreciate your response i think it could be even more engaging if you shared a personal experience related to the topic this might encourage more authentic interaction and elevate the discussion quality engage instructors without calling out specific students you might raise your concerns about the discussion quality with your instructors suggest incorporating more interactive elements or requiring posts to include personal insights and experiences humor in your posts inject some humor into your own posts to make them more enjoyable to read it might inspire others to do the same and make the discussions more lively form a study group create a study group with classmates who share your commitment to genuine discussion this way you can have more meaningful interactions outside the discussion boards remember the quality of your learning experience is what matters most focus on getting the most out of your education and take pride in knowing you re doing things the right way and when all else fails a good sense of humor can make a world of difference']
