Name: 71_learning_personalized_platform_feedback
Representative_Docs: ['our education system often fails to address the unique needs and strengths of each student leading to confusion and misaligned career choices current methods are not sufficient in helping students identify their true potential and match it with suitable career paths education should be more than just a means to a job it should be a journey of self discovery growth and alignment with one s passions yet the reality is that many students find themselves lost pressured by societal expectations and not guided by their own true interests they face a confusing maze of choices often without the tools to navigate it effectively this proposal offers a vision for a more compassionate and tailored approach to education leveraging ai and personalized assessments to guide students throughout their education a recent study at the university of tartu reveals a startling truth of students believe that having knowledge about careers is vital for cultivating genuine interest and highlight that loving one s career is crucial for motivation and satisfaction the current educational framework is failing these students unable to provide the individualized guidance they desperately need imagine emma a high school senior who shines in math and science despite her talents she feels pushed toward a business career perceived as more prestigious rather than exploring fields like engineering or data science where her true potential lies then there s john a college freshman who harbors a deep passion for creative writing but is hesitant to pursue journalism due to societal pressure he s left wondering how his unique skills could translate into a fulfilling career in writing and media consider an aspiring economist who might need to enhance their mathematical skills through additional coursework without proper guidance they may not realize these adjustments are necessary to excel in their chosen field the problem with modern education students today face a multitude of challenges due to a lack of personalized guidance our current systems provide generic advice ignoring the individual strengths and interests that make each student unique many students make career choices based on parental expectations or societal norms rather than their own passions and skills the absence of early and continuous guidance leaves them in the dark about their career options and the paths that truly resonate with their abilities and dreams the proposed solution we envision a comprehensive framework for assessment that starts early and continues throughout a student s life this framework includes evaluating academic strengths personal interests and career aspirations while also considering parental expectations and inspirations it calls for the creation of a visual tool like a radar chart or matrix diagram that we can provide at the end of school year as paper handouts beside the student s report card to help students and their families see how their skills and interests align with potential careers to further support students we propose the development of an ai driven career guidance system this platform would analyze students academic performance skills and interests to recommend suitable career paths it would offer insights into job roles necessary skills market trends and educational pathways while tracking progress and providing ongoing feedback implementation strategy to make this vision a reality we propose a multifaceted approach that starts with pilot programs in a diverse range of schools these pilots will test and refine our assessment tools and ai driven career guidance system feedback from students parents and educators will be crucial for fine tuning our approach before we expand it nationwide central to our strategy is the integration of regular assessments into the students academic routine quarterly and semester report cards will not only measure academic performance but also track progress toward career aspirations these assessments will provide a clear picture of where students excel and identify areas for further development for instance a student who shows exceptional talent in art might have their work showcased in local museums or community galleries a student with a flair for mathematics could be encouraged to participate in math olympiads or problem solving competitions for older students opportunities for early internships or apprenticeships will be made available based on their interests and skills a high school student passionate about architecture could be paired with architecture firms for internships gaining practical experience and professional connections similarly students with a talent for fine arts might be guided to participate in art exhibitions or collaborate with established artists the goal is to ensure that every student s unique talents are recognized and nurtured through tailored opportunities and experiences this guidance will help students explore their interests in real world contexts providing them with valuable experience and insights into potential career paths to support this initiative we will develop a global database of teachers and career counselors with hierarchical access levels this database will facilitate resource sharing progress tracking and effective guidance comprehensive training will be provided to educators to help them implement the new system and support students in making informed decisions about their future embedding regular assessments and opportunities into the educational framework we aim to create a dynamic and responsive system that helps students excel in their areas of interest and prepares them for successful and fulfilling careers expected benefits the impact of this proposal will be profound students will gain clarity about their strengths and how to align their studies with their career goals the guidance will alleviate uncertainty and stress providing clear paths to follow educational experiences will be better matched to students aspirations and personal interests additionally involving parents in the process will ensure that educational paths are aligned with family values and expectations we urge educational authorities school administrators and policymakers to embrace this vision by supporting a personalized learning framework and ai driven career Name: 73_cover_cover letter_letter_letters
Representative_Docs: ['i ve never used gpt to write cover letters but my successful cover letters always incorporated key words of position descriptions into the text of the letter and my cv that shouldn t be viewed negatively i m seeing job descriptions that directly warn applicants not to use gpt or similar for their applications why not do that and then if you still spot the use you can toss them when i hire i care very little about cover letters they seem like relics cv an interview reference check that really should be sufficient these days if i really needed a writing sanple i d ask them to do one as part of the interview process', 'there is a difference between having an llm assist with editing and tailoring the cover letter a super charged grammarly and having the llm write it from whole cloth the question to be asked is did you write the cover letter and have another entity assist with editing or did you have the other entity write the cover letter for you be it a career coach or llm the first is acceptable likewise it doesn t matter if some other human wrote it for you or if an llm wrote it the second is at issue if it is your creative thoughts that went into writing the cover letter and deciding what is in it and what is not then there shouldn t be any issue additionally the telltale fingerprints of an llm writing it won t be there the trick is to use the llm as an impartial editor rather than a generator have it tell you what types of things need to be looked at typos grammatical and punctuation errors inconsistent sentences that were left unfinished and then with its advice you edit it and repeat as necessary with that process the llm never wrote a line for you and its signature would not be in the writing', 'i don t suggest people just to take the chatgpt output directly you can use prompt engineering and manual tweaking to make a really well done cover letter that is way easier than to write custom letter from scratch for every position you are applying to also the common advice for cover letters is that you do tailor them the particular position in the current job market where ai is being used to filter your resume cover letter you re shooting yourself in the foot if you re not using ai to leverage key words and tailor made cover letters if you can tell it s written by ai you re using it wrong you can exclude words that are commonly associated with chatgpt and other llm s']

Name: 76_learn_console_projects_react
Representative_Docs: ['stop spending so much time watching tutorials and start building projects you ve heard this advice a lot but you ask yourself am i ready the answer is probably yes as a beginner other learn about git github and why source control aka saving your code is important and learn a few commands such as changing directory listing files and making a new directory from here choose a than an ai made as few errors as a human teacher would that change your mind they are not a replacement for all of the learning that occurs when one crafts independent analysis in written form the point of an essay or a presentation in a school setting is not for the teacher to read what a computer thinks about something i completely agree with this but it s beside the point imo as for the list of hallucinations this technology is progressing rapidly and it makes far fewer errors than it did even six months ago it is the nature of every neural net to hallucinate sometimes including our own brain we normally call it misremembering or being mistaken but it s a distinction with no functional difference i would encourage you to try the current models and see where they fall short', 'one would hope that a human teacher or professor was not hallucinating while creating their lesson plans and delivering their lessons while ai is here to stay k students should not be using generative ai because most of them do not have enough foundational knowledge to discern relevant output from ai hallucinations high school and college students should be exposed to ai in an educational setting to learn about the promise as well as the pitfalls of these tools however these are tools that can be used to save time they are not a replacement for all of the learning that occurs when one crafts independent analysis in written form the point of an essay or a presentation in a school setting is not for the teacher to read what a computer thinks about something the value is in the synthesis of information and conclusions drawn by the students who are learning how to think for themselves and how to analyze information some of the many pitfalls with sources ai hallucinations occur when an ai model generates information that isn t grounded in reality this often happens with generative ai models like openai s gpt or image generation tools the ai might fabricate responses make up facts or produce images that don t correspond to real world data while these errors might seem trivial in casual applications hallucinations can lead to severe consequences in business healthcare and other critical sectors avivah litan vp analyst at gartner explains hallucinations are completely fabricated outputs from large language models even though they represent completely made up facts the llm output presents them with confidence and authority generative ai driven chatbots can fabricate any factual information from names dates and historical events to quotes or even code hallucinations are common enough that openai actually issues a warning to users within chatgpt stating that chatgpt may produce inaccurate information about people places or facts the challenge for users is to sort through what information is true and what isn t preventing issues with generative open source technologies can prove challenging some notable examples of ai hallucination include google s bard chatbot incorrectly claiming that the james webb space telescope had captured the world s first images of a planet outside our solar system microsoft s chat ai sydney admitting to falling in love with users and spying on bing employees meta pulling its galactica llm demo in after it provided users inaccurate information sometimes rooted in prejudice while many of these issues have since been addressed and resolved it s easy to see how even in the best of circumstances the use of ai tools can have unforeseen and undesirable consequences']

Name: 81_reading_scene_read_quizzes
Representative_Docs: ['i find in my shakespeare class that the students most enjoyed classes are when we re enact a scene but do it in modern language we go through a play scene by scene together in class reading aloud and for the really consequential scenes after we have read it as written we reenact the scene allowing each character to express the sentiments of the play in whatever language they would like to use the banquet scene is so fun to do this way i find this helps their understanding and grasp of the concepts behind the play immeasurably at year end we also pick a scene to produce for family where we do costumes and memorized and quite condensed lines and just by doing that the students start picking up all these nuanced linguistic gems that even i hadn t picked up on i highly recommend role playing the important scenes making sure that you have contextualized the scene for them beforehand so they know the dynamics at play can t chatgpt any of that d also as a suggestion for the home reading assignments of a play perhaps could they instead watch a stage production of it one that is accurately rendered while they read along with their own script that seems to really help my younger pre high school shakespeare students especially', 'to have them actually read quizzes don t do discussion boards or notes or anything else just do quizzes and quizzes only short timed quizzes where they can t look stuff up and ideally on non plot points i actually quiz on footnotes frequently since they are essential to understanding the text what are antipodes nothingness is frequently discussed by hamlet when he speaks to ophelia what is nothing referencing etc if they are reading carefully they ll know how to answer if they re not they ll learn quickly that they need to read carefully for papers the usual anti ai fare have an ai policy in your syllabus don t be afraid to confront and fail students check their version history etc i am instituting a new policy next semester that using a tripartite thesis the kind that you use in paragraph essays and that chatgpt utilizes will get them off their grade automatically they shouldn t be using it in college anyway and this way they ll be forced to learn to write more nuanced arguments finally yeah definitely watch shakespeare together watch titus andronicus in the first few weeks that ll get their attention we ll watch at least half of the assigned plays in their entirety during the semester i tell them it is important because this is how you are supposed to experience shakespeare and that if you are only reading it it would be like you are only reading the script for a movie i use globe player our institution has a subscription to watch stage productions even before i did this i would include links to easily access them on canvas and about half of the class would watch at least parts of them on their own because it helped them understand the text better', 'one of the things i ve started doing i mostly teach online is to reframe the way i do discussion posts rather than have the students answer questions i require them to write higher order questions how or why questions based on the readings author or title of source i assign between pages a week history the question based approach to the discussion posts has really helped facilitate reading i was even surprised this week when i had students writing questions based on the hardest article i had them read this is a level course the article was definitely a grad level article i intentionally assign a few readings above grade level because reading things we struggle to understand actually helps facilitate growth in reading skills as long as it s not too far above of course this is the first time i ve used a question based approach with discussion boards and i m actually pretty impressed by how thoroughly the students have been reading i also have them do a page primary source analysis every week and they have page analytic essays i only had a few students use ai and once called out for it i have it in my syllabus that i use copyleaks for ai detection and consider its use equivalent to plagiarism those few students dropped the course the course might be a bit of an outlier though as students who take summer classes tend to be a bit more proactive about their learning due to the rapid pace but it s also a course on a subject that most people find fascinating the history of witchcraft and heresy in europe all of that said i wasn t teaching years ago so i don t know the intricacies of how much has changed i have seen textbooks and syllabi from that time period though and my immediate thought upon seeing them is always students today couldn t handle that level of work it doesn t help that k has moved away from teaching grammar so it s really like we get students with writing and reading skills that are middle school level that s not surprising when you consider that the average reading level in the u s is th grade it s like college has become high school because high school doesn t teach kids the skills they need to succeed in college pretty frustrating']

Name: 82_parents_school_policy_kid
Representative_Docs: ['tldr summary a massachusetts high school senior s parents are suing his teacher and school district after he was punished for using ai to create an outline for a history essay the lawsuit claims the student didn t break any rules as the ai policy was added after the incident the punishments including a low grade and exclusion from the national honor society have impacted his college applications the case could set a legal precedent as ai use in schools grows the family seeks to reverse the penalties and clear the student s academic record before college deadline', 'the school has asked the court to dismiss the suit and has laid out a strong case for doing so among other arguments they include these facts the school had a clear and detailed policy on academic integrity which included explicit limits and guidelines about the use of ai students were given that information in a dedicated class that this student attended which included a powerpoint presentation on ai use and policies and a handout parents were given the same information at parents night the student admitted using ai in multiple ways in violation of the policy and agreed to the consequences a detention and opportunity to redo the project for a grade of d along with deferral of admission to the national honor society he was later admitted parents met with administration and accepted consequences the case for the school looks very strong and their ai policy is well thought out', 'i have no idea about the evaluation of projects for this particular prize at emory but at my current university and former universities a lot of these incubator things and campus level research innovation awards are judged by whatever panel of assorted profs and sometimes administrators they can guilt into taking on the service burden of review in most cases where there are risks involved in a project there are other mechanisms that evaluate those risks e g human subjects research is reviewed by irb so the panel of non subject area experts can go about rewarding interesting ideas without worrying too much about endorsing unethical or illegal projects if someone were given a research innovation prize but the research ultimately failed to get required irb approval for example the research wouldn t go forward but nobody would sue the university about that we don t have institutional ethics or legal review boards for ai research innovation that i m aware of the lawsuit here states that the judges for the entrepreneurship award were business school grad students and alumni i m sure the panel had the business acumen to vet the financial viability of a project but probably didn t have the programming computational linguistics campus it or legal expertise to fully vet the ip ai and university computing data policy elements of something like this it seems really clear that the students were given the wrong impression about the overall viability of their project based on acclaim for the business side of it but the students probably should have been smart enough to realize that b school students and alumni don t know the ins and outs of canvas apis and it policies they probably should have been gently informed of the computing security data management or ip issues at play by those unfortunate souls forced to be bad cop rather than suspended if they were gently informed of policy violations or questions regarding the legality of their data use and they persisted in developing the project and using university resources in ways that violate policies then maybe their semester long suspension isn t so crazy the reporting and the lawsuit treats the business school the it department the honor code office and various administrators all as a monolithic university that made a whiplash inducing about face but in reality there were probably many layers of interaction with different campus units between the initial award and the honor code review that introduced nuance i suspect there is more to the story than has been reported especially given that the article editorializes as far as ai tools go it seems innocuous that s a big claim to make without knowing more about how it handles the data it ingests']

Name: 84_journals_journal_conferences_publish

Name: 85_energy_consumption_water_centre
Representative_Docs: ['again this is nothing half the article said shit we should already know like the earth is water no way the it said these vowing to limit their water consumption which is kinda what yall wanted in the first place the only piece of data in here is their rate of water consumption cool awesome but what does it mean in context who s the worst offender and what rate are they consuming who s the best shining example we should be modeling consumption after what s the average consumption what is a sustainable amount of water consumption they were already using water to cool off their data centers so what s the percentage increase since implementing ai what s the increase among the different companies what s that increase compared to global water consumption is it even that big of an impact none of which are answered all of which are probably good pieces of info to know before jumping on the ai is hurting our environment train this is quite literally a sensationalist propaganda piece', 'yes the environmental costs of ai are a problem generating an image with dall e uses the same amount of energy as it takes to charge your phone from however these companies have an incentive to improve energy efficiency in pre training and inference which are expensive processes fortunately reducing energy costs has the dual benefit of reducing emissions there are companies developing more efficient inference methods already ai consumers also need to learn that not every task needs a state of the art ai light models or none at all work just fine for most situations reducing emissions ai may also spark transitions to green energy since more capacity is needed even nuclear energy may see a renaissance which when coupled with transition to ev may be a necessity i discuss this in my lectures on ai i make sure students understand holistically the implications of this technology and teach them how to think for themselves not what to think ai is currently a part of the climate problem yes but ignoring the reality that it s part of our lives now will not help it may be more comfortable to dismiss this reality and hide behind a single sound bite but i want my students to have more education on ai not less so they understand the nuance and make decisions for themselves after this discussion some students decide they don t want to use ai anymore and others are motivated to learn more about the externalities of tech', 'the massive energy demands of ai are undoing all of our progress in fighting climate change psa this isn t even remotely true breaking it down there s the energy cost of training and there s the energy cost of inference training is a pretty much once off thing and it is a huge consumer of power it s done in a single data centre in a single location it takes many months but you can choose any data centre in the world and the cost of electricity is a significant factor openai went with a data centre in des moines iowa and it was likely powered by wind and hydro because that s where of iowa s power comes from if you want to argue that we should never train another large language model after gpt because future energy requirements are going to be too huge which is what a very small number of people are predicting that s an ok ish position to take but i think there s a better thing to advocate for and that s no training of language models using fossil fuel powered electricity it would mean ai vendors would have to train a model more slowly suspending when renewables weren t available and resuming when they were it would be annoying but no big deal for the ai model vendors to comply with this it s the kind of thing that could be controlled by regulation that would prevent future environmental impact i have a student right now working on model training that is aware of its own environmental impact and can move itself to better locations as required inference the energy cost of running a model that has already been trained is pretty tiny openai s total energy demand worldwide is below mw if openai went pirate they could buy three ships off royal caribbean and power the entire world s ai inference needs and still have some power left over a trained model is small it fits on a usb stick pretty easily and is easy to spread around data centres at openai s scale you can negotiate a cheaper deal from a data centre operator if you can shape your energy demand to match the local energy production so what companies typically do is called follow the sun load balancing which sounds exactly like it is i haven t confirmed that openai is doing this but it would be really surprising if they weren t the only reason they wouldn t be was because they had some deals with data centre providers that had hydro or wind deals that were cheaper than solar if you want to fight climate change do something about fossil fuel powered cars and or stop flying in planes ai isn t the problem']

Name: 87_assignment_paper_report_student
Representative_Docs: ['here are my recommendations ask to meet with the student ask them to tell you how they wrote their project ask them to describe specific things that the paper cites that you think it is unlikely that they understand if all else fails tell them that their response has much in common with ai generated content and ask them explicitly if they used ai when writing their paper additionally you can look to see if there are other more objective indications of ai that you can act upon like citations or articles that do not actually exist some students will fess up to ai use in which case you can act accordingly if you meet with a student and they seem utterly clueless about the content of their paper that is reasonable grounds for a bad grade and an academic integrity report if nothing else the student may be scared when they get an email asking to meet and know that professors are on to them going forward you might consider requiring students to write all assignments in google docs that they give you editing access to you can then see if a student appears to be writing the assignment organically or is copy pasting which is consistent with ai use the draftback chrome extension can help with this this is not foolproof but is one more tool in the toolkit this is a difficult situation and many institutions are not providing faculty with the necessary support and backup i encourage you to do what you can this term and adjust assignments going forward', 'i just finished grading a virtual stack of papers the last one is presenting a problem i m pretty sure it s been written with ai as it is mostly generic sentences that sound nice but say absolutely nothing the thing that fooled me initially is that there are citations to our class sources a requirement for the assignment thrown in throughout the paper other ai papers i ve seen usually have no references or just hallucinate sources however the sentences that mention the sources are also vague and give no indication that the student actually read anything other than the title maybe the first paragraph or two the lms ai checker says ai writing for everything but the title page and reference section no other paper received an ai score that high most are at i do say in my syllabus that unauthorized help on an assignment could result in a grade of now even if this isn t an ai paper the grade is still not going to be great because of how generic it is the student definitely did not meet the assignment objective but it wouldn t be a the student is supposed to graduate this semester so i know they are going to fight this hard and i m not sure how accurate the ai checkers are do i just assign the low grade and move on or should i contact the student and our office of academic integrity about this', 'rant please i beg report ai use and plagiarism to your university s academic integrity or student conduct office i had cases last semester and reported them all resulting in zeros for the course meanwhile other professors in my department and even my own department chair just issue a slap on the wrist a zero for the assignment or an option for a re do even if ai and plagiarism were clearly discussed in class as prohibited prohibited in the assignment instructions and clear penalties laid out in the syllabus i know of one student who was caught twice previously but not reported to academic integrity and then tried the same thing in my class the unethical students will continue to do it because they can get away with it if there s not some shared record of this happening then there will be no consequences and ai use will continue to run rampant and it s not just undergraduates our grad program is having the same problem caveats i say this with the privilege of a tt position at an r that largely supports faculty decisions over ai use and penalties for ai use i understand some academic integrity offices are not great and don t back up the professors i also realize that if you are in a more precarious position an ai accusation could lead to a disgruntled student tanking your evals it also is added workload submitting ai plagiarism cases to academic integrity office but a little solidarity could go a long way to cutting down on the amount of robot written papers we grade']

Name: 92_programming_coding_age_job
Representative_Docs: ['i mean it just depends i think he didn t approach it properly in my opinion what i would suggest is learning programming learn how computers work learn the math and you will be fine just be prepared go study all the time thing in tech is if you don t maintain your skill or grow you for sure will lose it it s just one of those things you gotta keep learning if you think you can enjoy learning for the rest of your life that s all you need i have a few friends a bit younger than you they want to learn to be programmer but for the money the status but they want it fastest way so they can earn money nah you gonna fail this way if you truly are willing to keep a flow of constant learning and you enjoy this just start and don t worry about ai these developers know nothing about ai if someone shares their opinion on ai making devs obsolete trust me they know nothing how an ai works under the hood they just yappy yappy', 'hmmm all coding jobs are about building things even data analytics and machine learning to be honest that s the part of the part many of us enjoy in either a data analytics or machine learning job a big part of the job is writing code to clean up data and get it in the right format i wouldn t call it the fun part of the job but it s what you ll be spending tons of time doing like imagine you re building something like a classifier for news articles someone s collected urls of thousands of articles and categorized them as left leaning or right leaning you want to feed those to a model but the model only wants the text of the article you need to strip out anything else that might bias it like the name of the website even something like links to other sections of the article or the way they credit authors could give away which publisher it was from then some articles have ads in the middle or paywalls you need to get past all of that needs to be coded note that machine learning is even more math heavy you d have to take college level linear algebra at a minimum plus probability statistics they probably require advanced calculus as a prerequisite at most schools again not trying to scare you or tell you not to do it just being realistic about what the job actually entails day to day the reality is most programming jobs are extremely boring but that s not necessarily a bad thing i spend hours a week writing extremely tedious boring code to get data from point a to point b i do all of that so that because the end result is worth it and i get a lot of satisfaction out of getting to that answer or to that final product', 'everyone can learn to code at any age and become a programmer hard part is thinking like an engineer if we are talking about ai being able to build products without human intervention that s only possible up to the limit of code input we feed to that specific model it won t innovate for us it will only copy and paste for us build what build how i think these are the things we need to understand as engineers yes ai can build stuff but usually the output is not optimal yet when you need innovation you need human intervention and an engineering mindset that s quite hard to replicate until we achieve agi and even then it will still hold its value if you want to become a software engineer be very careful about the distinction between a programmer and an engineer you can program something after getting couple of courses but thinking like an engineer will be the harder and surely more valuable part you might get laid off in next years if you are just a programmer engineers will stick around your friend here seems like a programmer with years of experience i wouldn t count on his word ability to learn new tools and implement new solutions continuously is what makes an engineer valuable not ability to code about age actually age is quite irrelevant here but i understand that the older you get more risk averse you become so this decision needs to generate enough income that s more about showcasing selling your skills you need to understand that python or other programming languages and software are just tools that engineers use to build a product in the most efficient way tools can change and they will change you need to focus on learning how to learn and implementing quickly with efficiency that s how you can have a job even after ai or agi or whatever innovation we will have hopefully you won t need a job and build your own income solutions at some point']

Representative_Docs: ['i majored in humanities and work with cutting edge ai the specific work i do didn t exist three years ago so you couldn t really career prep for it my team is about humanities majors stem business majors who accidentally tripped and fell into data science the stem grads all have at least years of graduate study so it definitely wasn t immediate income cs grads are mostly useless to us because their knowledge is outdated the minute they graduate and so many allegedly cs degrees are actually just programming degrees the thing that everyone on our team has regardless of background is an absolutely voracious curiosity and the intellectual capacity to upskill in new realms of knowledge you need to have learned how to learn and how to be a lifelong learner which are skills i think the humanities teach better than anything else not that there aren t plenty of polymaths in biology or anywhere else the thing is you can t really study for the next big in demand skill you can follow the trends of prior years and watch everyone pack into cs degrees or law degrees and oversaturate the market you can guess what to spend years studying but the next big thing career wise only barely exists right now and nobody s studying for it you re going to have to learn it on the job by being the person who can learn it on the job the other skills that i think matter a lot for what i do are being an excellent communicator hard to get headcount if no one in management understands what you re doing and hard to collaborate with colleagues if you re a shitty communicator which i think is a big focus in humanities and not being afraid to ask very stupid questions out loud which i think is more a matter of personal temperament where you re more desperate to know something than you are to seem smart stem is the one true branch of knowledge guys are never going to be able to cut it because they don t have the intellectual humility to admit they re lost', 'there are very few single discipline jobs so i d recommend systems as a component of your specialization e g distributed hpc supercomputing databases graphics signal processing digital logic hardware architecture security devops and languages would supplement and inform your decisionmaking pure systems programming nowadays is mostly of the middleware sort unless you re in the research end of things in which case super high performance computing and hardware design are both reasonable fields embedded may involve some of it but most platforms already have some sort of software base linux connective tissue mostly but knowing the fundamentals means you ll recognize the patterns and be able to implement them yourself whether or not on bare metal knowing how to hand off between sync and async knowing how to do multithreaded or locality aware scheduling knowing how and when to run in kernel mode knowing how to deal with boundary interactions safely knowing what all the stupid unixy terms to man are knowing how to model predict analyze and correct larger scale behaviors etc all make life easier in any programming adjacent pursuits there are also enormous differences in how you get at the metal depending on what shape it s in distributed multithreaded multiprocess and heterogeneous disciplines are all very different but knowing how all the moving parts work and how to move them means you ll be able to make full obscene use of the hardware and eke out performance ime ai is kinda shit at details or knowing things that very few people know so expertise and hands on experience are vital if you want to compete also somebody needs to make the ai run and it s expensive to run so making it run faster cheaper will make you popular at parties not the fun sort of parties at first then if you climb the corporate ladder high enough fun if you leave early', 'i have a mechatronics degree and work in plant automation as an engineer i m not sure if either of these are going to be the perfect solution for you mechatronics errs more on the side of electrical controls and process automation which while robots can be involved is almost never a focal point you will likely learn your way around a plc r plc and develop an idea of how the industrial processes work think simple valve operation pipe and instrumentation diagrams basic mechanics you may learn to write simple programs to control processes design user interfaces hmi and maybe even get to play with some robot arms think standard six axis arms but i cannot stress this enough you would not be building robots you would be moving robots if your school is well funded you might be programming the robots to do little routines or even basic maintenance to start i am working my way through a computer science degree currently so i m not an expert in this field i develop software in an industrial setting so i do still think it s relevant machine learning is not going to be something that lends itself toward practical robotics if you re lucky maybe you can weasel your way into a startup making funky robotic companions somewhere but don t know however image recognition and vision systems are always bustling so yeah you can definitely still do something with it as in you could have a camera recognize that a part is bad and have a robot arm remove it from production if this is truly something you want to pursue then i honestly would just recommend computer science if you can find a program with embedded systems as a focus most of the dirty work of robotics is done on a software level glean some knowledge of servo motors and encoders sure yes you will need it but at the end of the day most of the meat is going to be in the programming if you are able to try and pursue a minor in something like physics maybe take a course in cad if it suits you you must understand how these things orient themselves i d bet there are free online courses on this stuff out there somewhere by now all of my formal training is from manufacturers which may not be available to you if you have no in to the industry unfortunately i hope this is helpful and others that know more than me can correct me please don t be discouraged and even if your career does not push you into the exact position you want immediately never stop learning don t be dissuaded from applying for jobs you re not qualified for or asking for help when you don t know something it s a fulfilling path']

Name: 97_journal_journals_manuscript_author
Representative_Docs: ['i recently created a video where i explore the evolving role of ai specifically gpt in the writing and co authorship of scientific publications there s a common misconception that ai is doing all the heavy lifting but in reality ai works under human direction in the video i discuss whether ai like gpt should be credited as a co author or if it s merely a tool in the hands of the writer i also touch on the reliability of ai generated links and the potential issues we might face in the future for those of you involved in academia research or ai i d love to hear your thoughts on this topic do you think ai deserves a place as a co author in academic papers what challenges or opportunities do you see with the increasing use of ai in scientific publications watch the video here read example blog let s discuss', 'op if you insist on doing this make sure to check very carefully if the journal allows this many publishers have policies which restrict forbid or discourage use of generative ai for writing papers and you don t want to run afoul of this if the particular journal has such a policy in place it seems like most journals are leaning towards you can use it but you must disclose that fact so it s probably fine but check and make sure so you don t accidentally commit an ethics violation', 'i don t think ai should be an author specially in its current state description in methods would depend how it was used if it was only used to improve grammar on the manuscript i would not cite it in the methods as i equate it to the spelling checker in ms word if it was used as part of the analysis it should be included with the specific instructions inputs and outputs included probably in an annex if used for lit review for the intro or discussion i think human authors have the final responsibility probably here the issue is more around research ethics purely ai work in its current state would likely result in inconsistencies that should be caught by editors and peer reviewers although someone could potentially push a manuscript through this the authors lack of domain around the topic would become self evident in the real world conferences discussions etc']

Name: 98_ml_swe_mle_roles
Representative_Docs: ['your masters in ai ml and projects are valuable but you re right that many companies prioritize practical experience focus on leveraging your swe background as a bridge to ml roles look for positions that blend software engineering with machine learning such as ml infrastructure or mlops roles these can be great entry points into the ml field allowing you to apply your existing skills while learning ml specific practices on the job don t give up on your ml aspirations or settle for a job you dislike keep refining your skills work on more complex ml projects and consider contributing to open source ml projects to gain practical experience network with professionals in the field and attend ml meetups or conferences as for those tricky interview questions about large scale deployment or production experience be honest about your limitations but emphasize your ability to learn quickly and adapt by the way i m on the team that created interviews chat a tool designed to help you navigate these challenging interview scenarios and boost your confidence in ml related discussions', 'hi i m graduating from my ms cs specializing in ai soon and have offers as a backend swe at a top startup and an ml engineer at an earlier stage startup i have a good amount of ml research and course experience as i ve been recruiting this cycle i originally wanted to pursue only ml engineering but found that there were very little entry level openings as many required prior ml engineering experience or at least some backend experience thus i turned to new grad backend roles although recently i was able to land a more junior ml engineering position in general it seems that ml engineering roles typically seek engineers with backend experience as well as some ml experience from either research or industry currently i m more inclined toward the backend swe role since it is at a more reputable rocket ship like company does anyone have insight on whether it will be feasible to eventually transition to ml engineering if i take the backend swe position which will likely entail some llm integration work as well since i have past ml research experience as well as a ms degree in ai', 'i got a bachelors in cs and started working as a back end dev at a start up i was always interested in ai and taught myself a lot of the theory when i was in high school and college in the course of my work as a back end dev i identified a use case for ml and pursued it in my own time i was eventually able to get approval to transition to the project full time because it was very valuable the project turned out to be very successful and i got my title changed to mle i m now a senior for other devs looking to transition i would recommend the following get really good at ml on your own time this could mean pursuing a masters degree or just self studying try to move into an ml adjacent dev role this could include things like backend dev data engineer data analyst it helps if you have access to a lot of data as part of your role even better if you can get close to people already doing types of ml you find interesting even better if it s a small company with less rigidly defined roles in addition to your normal duties try to do some small ml projects with the data you have access to or offer to help existing ml specialists with engineering work you need to prove to your boss and coworkers that you understand ml and are capable of generating value with it after a while you transition to doing mostly ml work you can make the case to have your title changed to mle or similar you can also apply to ml jobs and emphasize your previous ml work as experience to summarize i would not recommend applying directly to ml roles with no experience nobody is going to hire a random mobile dev with no ml experience find a role adjacent to ml and then get experience that way']

Name: 99_policy_zero_ai policy_quiz
Representative_Docs: ['i have started a new policy for my courses that have written assignments that are not in person i teach one course outside my home institution and they have a different course set up that includes a weekly discussion in that class i have a zero tolerance ai policy where if ai is suspected the student receives an automatic zero for the assignment if they want to contest the findings they have the option to complete a proctored quiz that i have made over the content as an alternative it does create extra work on my end however since i implemented it in this class i have had two instances of ai and neither student opted for the quiz and took the zero', 'if you tried to fail half the students for that reason you d be forced to back off that s why i withdraw them instead with blessings from my department first because w s hurt a lot in texas students can only have ws ever it can also impact financial aid because if they go below x number of courses in a semester they lose their aid both sting quite a bit i let them know from day one that i don t tolerate ai use and will withdraw them for academic misconduct and it s pretty easy to detect when the ding dongs don t even bother to take out the ai as a large language model statement before submitting or use words they can t even define when asked i give them one warning if i catch them and if they do it again they re gone this is outlined in the syllabus with a strikes you re out policy second at my institution faculty have the ability to administratively withdraw students i type in a name and they re gone my department division encourages using administrative w s versus giving out f s so i do do i withdraw half my students no i think it averages around for my s and for my s obviously not all are ai misconduct withdraws but that number is steadily going up thankfully all my courses are mandatory core classes so enrollment isn t ever a concern', 'i get down voted to oblivion every time i mention that i don t automatically report every suspicion of ai use but this is exactly why i run my course the way i do i d rather cheaters get a grade for nothing than put just one honest student through what she went through after her work was flagged olmsted says she became obsessive about avoiding another accusation she screen recorded herself on her laptop doing writing assignments she worked in google docs to track her changes and create a digital paper trail she even tried to tweak her vocabulary and syntax honest students should not have to deal with that shit and better yet it s not even like the ai cheaters are getting a pass in my course they re still being graded on their writing and flat formulaic language doesn t get them much and mistakes with documenting sources or using fake sources is even worse on their grade my rubric and policy structure handles all that without ever needing to mention ai i m sure it s not the same for every discipline but i m able to fairly easily create a rubric and assignment prompt that cannot be done well with chatgpt in other words someone who relies on llms to generate most of their writing is probably going to fail or earn a very low grade in my course and the number of cs ds and fs i ll be assigning at the end of this semester prove it meanwhile i am not spending countless hours fucking around with ai detectors playing detective trying to manipulate confessions out of students and writing reports']

Name: 100_degree_engineering_career_science
Representative_Docs: ['hello everyone i m currently at a crossroads in my career and i ve been struggling with finding the right direction i ve been studying mechanical engineering for about seven years now but my issues aren t with grades or the difficulty of the topics while being slow i m doing well academically the problem is that i m no longer sure if this is even the right field for me to give you some background i initially chose this path because i naively believed in sustainable engineering projects such as the ocean cleanup and because i wanted to contribute to society through technology my parents also encouraged me to pursue engineering selling me on the ideas of high tech innovation silicon valley dreams and the belief that engineering drives societal progress i am from germany and at that time was not aware that most jobs would link me to the automotive or fossil industries that i already disliked at that time initially i focused on design and simulation working with cad and fea but i found these areas incredibly boring over time and didn t see them as particularly innovative i then specialized in controls only to realize that opportunities in this field were largely reserved for electrical engineers i later moved to vibration and dynamics but found the work to be driven more by academic curiosity in math than practical impactful outcomes many of my peers who pursued phds in this area ended up switching careers entirely moving into banking business consulting software development or dropping out altogether throughout my studies i was involved in various projects like a formula student team and work at an industrial research facility these experiences were often frustrating with projects failing and the work feeling monotonous even my roles as a student assistant didn t ignite much passion i continued into a master s program because it seemed like the logical next step but i still didn t have a clear vision of my future my attempts to specialize in controls didn t pan out and i even dabbled in machine learning only to find that it is mostly tech hype and that this is not an adequate qualification for a mechanical engineering masters degree over time i ve realized that much of mechanical engineering isn t as innovative or high tech as i initially thought i see more cutting edge work happening in software data analysis and sensing technologies this realization combined with my growing concern for sustainability has made me question my place in this field i feel conflicted about working in industries like automotive and aerospace which contribute to climate change and i don t believe that innovation alone can offset the environmental damage these industries cause i am really concerned about limate change and enviromental protection but i see those problems as societal problems nowadays if i were to stay in meche i would like to work for trains bicycles or renewable energy all but the last category usually have absolutely no job offerings posted it is extremely rare to get into these fields it ppers all to be about automotive aviation or chemical oil and gas industries that i dislike in fact there seem to be very few job openings for meche in general particularly in specialized areas like fea this also has to do with the decline of the german car industry and because of nationwide bans for hiring at those companies environmental engineering on the other hand seems to offer more opportunities in germany with more job security as well but i have doubts about this path too the work i ve seen seems to involve a lot of basic excel tasks and following empirical rulebooks which feels very uncreative and not particularly scientific or innovative right now i m working in vibration analysis which feels repetitive and lacks creativity it s hard to imagine doing this kind of work for the rest of my career it is basically one experiment that we do on similar products over and over again i m starting to wonder if becoming an expert in any field inevitably leads to boring monotonous work or if this is a sign that i m in the wrong field entirely given all this i ve been seriously considering changing careers i ve thought about moving into areas like wastewater engineering or river engineering which seem more aligned with my desire to do work that benefits the public flooding and water management are becoming increasingly important issues and these areas seem to offer more meaningful work i ve also considered completely different paths like becoming a teacher maybe in physical education and math or even pursuing medicine to help people directly i m really at a crossroads and would appreciate any advice or experiences you all can share has anyone else been in a similar situation how did you find your way forward what keeps you motivated in your career especially if the work feels repetitive or lacks impact thanks in advance for your help thanks for reading', Representative_Docs: ['seriously i can t imagine spending hours and hours on an assignment and then having to redo the whole thing the teacher thinks it looks like ai neither can these kids because they re using ai', 'seriously my class had a very small simple easy paper to write solely on our own personal opinion on why crime happens everyone has an opinion on crime yet a lot of my classmates used ai to answer it they didn t even try to hide it were a damn intro class and they wrote their papers very complex and mentioned theories we haven t even covered in class oh man these people are not gonna do well in life lol', 'to reduce ai use in assignments consider these strategies educate on academic integrity discuss the importance of original work and the ethical implications of using ai redesign assignments create tasks that require critical thinking personal reflection or unique perspectives that ai can t easily replicate incorporate in class work use in class discussions or presentations to assess understanding in real time use technology tools employ plagiarism detection software or tools that can identify ai generated text build a relationship foster an environment where students feel comfortable discussing their struggles reducing the temptation to cheat provide support offer resources for research and writing skills to help students feel more capable in their work set clear expectations clearly outline what constitutes acceptable use of ai tools in your assignments implementing a combination of these approaches can help maintain academic integrity']

Name: 103_white_hidden_screen readers_readers
Representative_Docs: ['i ve said it before and i ll say it again forever all white text invisible dye ai tricks are inherently inaccessible and discriminatory for any students using assistive technology both with or without documented disabilities regardless of the instructor s intention versions that rely on white text on white backgrounds have the potential to entrap any students who use dark mode high contrast mode or a variety of accessibility tools like screen readers versions that rely on using images of text instead of actual text especially images with some kind of obscuring overlay are inaccessible to students using screen readers or other accessibility tools some instructors are desperate for a magic bullet that will make ai not exist anymore and allow them to use assessments that they haven t modified for a decade the only real solution is to change the way we assess student knowledge and skill development', 'how can i prevent this copy paste confusion happening going forward you can t it s a serious flaw in the trojan horse trick any students using any assistive technologies like screen readers immersive readers students that enable their web browser s dark mode or high contrast mode or students that have a workflow of copying and pasting the assignment instructions into their word document will all see your hidden white text and could potentially get caught in your trap it s an accessibility grievance waiting to happen i understand the desire to find an easy magic bullet to short circuit ai misuse without having to do any reflection on your assignment design but it s just not a good solution some assignments just need to be re designed split into multiple scaffolded steps adapted into something less writing oriented and more performance production oriented', 'yes this was my thought screen readers will read the whole thing even if it practically invisible to the rest of us let s not torture students who need assistive technology to hide text from a screen reader and display it visually use the aria hidden attribute and set it to true p aria hidden true visible text screen readers should ignore p i haven t tested hiding it using the hidden attribute but if it s hidden entirely ai might or might not read it and it would not show up when using dark mode or simple text reader view on some browsers p hidden Name: 104_essay_essays_grading_graders
Representative_Docs: ['my essay writing and grading system which i have been using in some format for over a decade seems to be handling the ai age fairly well to give an idea i do not give students prompts they have to come up with their own topics based upon the reading and class discussion and i am happy to help if they get stuck i give students detailed instructions about which things the essay must have in order to successfully complete the task we talk about it in class and i provide some sample essays with comments i give students the marking rubric which is quite detailed and stick to it when evaluating essays in theory one could use a llm to generate the various parts of the essay and put them together however this would not yet provide the logical structure needed for a successful essay and would still receive fairly low marks in practice students are usually not that motivated instead they ask the ai to write a paper on topic x some reading from the class or they find an essay prompt on the internet and ask it to write an essay on that topic unfortunately for the students the llm then generates an essay that has nothing to do with my rubric and is marked accordingly this means that students using this method will usually earn about of the available points this can make it tricky to pass my course i actually think it s kind of fun to mark suspected ai generated essays i leave comments pointing out the lack of a thesis repetitive or unclear language and logical inconsistencies in the argument i suspect that most students will not read these but if i can get through to somebody that ai cannot do what a human can do that s a win', 'friend time saving ai can quickly grade large batches of essays freeing up teachers time for other tasks like lesson planning or providing personalized feedback consistency ai can offer a more consistent grading process reducing the chance of bias or subjectivity in scoring immediate feedback students can get quick insights into their work helping them identify areas for improvement before the final submission foe lack of nuance ai might struggle with grading complex creative or nuanced arguments that require deep understanding and context over reliance teachers may rely too much on ai which could limit the depth of personal feedback that students need for growth errors while ai can be accurate it s not perfect and may misinterpret or fail to grasp certain points in an essay especially in more sophisticated writing in short ai graders are useful tools but they work best when used alongside human insight teachers can combine ai s efficiency with their own expertise to provide the most effective grading for more on how ai can assist students and educators check out this post top writing services for students your ultimate solution for academic success', 'grading essays especially for large classes can be a time consuming and tedious task naturally technological advancements like ai pique our interest as educators offering potential solutions to lighten the load however the question remains would you trust an ai to handle your essay grading recently i experimented with an ai essay grader accessible even to non tech savvy users like myself it performs the basic function of grading essays but also provides additional features like summarizing content detecting plagiarism identifying errors and even offering feedback the tool can even be customized based on your own grading rubrics is the ai essay grader a friend or foe for teachers']

making this their full time job the money they make is just a side benefit and trust me they do make a lot of it but their primary focus is always on their students you see the most important thing you need is a passion for what you want to create a course about and a genuine desire to make an impact that s it here is what i do i follow many people on instagram and many of them just want to help Name: 108_open access_publisher_book_access
Representative_Docs: ['apparently academic publishers have been licensing scholarly publications to ai companies to use the publications to train llms and to enable the llms to accurately cite the publications and locate the publications when the llms are used for search purposes i ve learned that several publishers have just been doing this without consent of their authors but a major exception is cambridge university press which is giving authors a choice to opt in you can read about it at this link i have to commend cambridge up for giving authors a choice and for presenting a reasoned argument for the press s position the gist of what they say in that link is that licensing the publications to ai companies gives authors and publishers a chance to try to set legal parameters on how the publications can be used by ai and it will also give a major boost to the discoverability of these publications since ai will be used to index and locate sources in the future here are their own words gen ai tools are likely to become an important component of how information is shared and discovered and access to high quality academic content may contribute to increasing the quality of these tools engaging constructively with gen ai providers means we can ensure authors rights are considered as these tools are developed encouraging and collaborating in lawful routes to content licensing should help to ensure fair remuneration for the use of authors work and allow us to set legal parameters on that use in many cases gen ai tools are being built that increase the discoverability and therefore the impact of the work we publish or being used as a component of existing licensing deals that are important for providing access to the content so i guess the questions i have for this subreddit are what are the dangers or downsides of licensing publications to ai what are the dangers or downsides of not licensing publications full disclosure i have a publication with cambridge up and i have to decide whether to opt in to this i have been rather concerned about ai technology in terms of pedagogy see my post history but i m unsure of what to make of ai in terms of academic publications and i thought i would ask if anyone has any special knowledge or informed speculation the way i understand it now is that even if i don t agree to opt in ai companies may end up scraping my publication anyway and just use its ideas without crediting me so one argument would be that since it will happen with or without me it s better for it to happen with me maybe i ll get something out of it this way and some credit for my work so that s the direction i m leaning toward but what are the downsides i m not seeing it s hard to know what you don t know what are these llms going to be capable of doing with my work in the future in the next decade will an llm be able to generate a follow up book to mine in under five minutes philosophically i sort of hate the idea of helping to train these things and by dong so possibly obviating the need for human thinkers in the future', 'hey everyone i have an exciting opportunity to publish my first book as open access and my university will cover the costs i love the idea of making my work freely accessible to the academic community but i have a few concerns and would really appreciate your input firstly how does making a book open access impact its sales i can t help but think that if the book is freely available online it might reduce the motivation for people to buy it have any of you faced this issue and how did it turn out for you i know that publishing articles open access often results in higher citations and greater reach but does this apply to books as well and would it outweigh the potential downside of lost sales another thing that s been on my mind is the rise of ai tools like chatgpt i m a bit worried about someone taking my open access content running it through an ai and potentially passing it off as their own i know this can happen with any published work but it feels like the risk might be higher with open access has anyone dealt with this and how did you handle it lastly besides the benefit of wider information sharing which i fully support are there other advantages you ve experienced from publishing open access any unexpected positives would be great to hear about thanks a bunch for your insights and advice', 'hey everyone i also posted this in academia sub but i m posting here as well to get more input i have a traditional book contract with a reputable publisher sage recently my university offered to cover the cost of publishing the book open access if i desire the book will still undergo peer review whether i publish traditionally or oa i love the idea of making my work freely accessible to the academic community but i have a few concerns and would really appreciate your input firstly how does making a book open access impact its sales i can t help but think that if the book is freely available online it might reduce the motivation for people to buy it have any of you faced this issue and how did it turn out for you i know that publishing articles open access often results in higher citations and greater reach which could mean more speaking engagement small media appearances consulting but all opinions welcome would it outweigh the potential downside of lost sales another thing that s been on my mind is the rise of ai tools like chatgpt i m a bit worried about someone taking my open access content running it through an ai and potentially passing it off as their own i know this can happen with any published work but it feels like the risk might be higher with open access has anyone dealt with this and how did you handle it lastly besides the benefit of wider information sharing which i fully support are there other advantages you ve experienced from publishing open access any unexpected positives would be great to hear about thanks a bunch for your insights and advice']

response especially with the gpt model it comes with the paid version it s absolutely great for uploading documents like textbook chapters and peer reviewed studies once you upload your documents you can ask coral ai any question and give it specific requirements that i want it to use such as analyzing a research paper while using methodology from the course textbook i often use the prompt my college assignment gives me the end result is that coral ai gives good answers that are broken down into bullet points with references to where it gathered its information in your resources which you can then expound on and incorporate into your papers cons the one thing i hate about coral is that it can t use weblinks it can only use document files which sucks when you need to use parts of your textbook which is often a protected e book that doesn t let you export it as a pdf however i did find a workaround if you need to use a webpage article with coral ai go to that webpage and select the print option this option probably varies depending on your browser once you select to print the page change the export to pdf essentially you can export a webpage or article as a pdf and then upload it to coral ai though this doesn t work for some of my school textbooks oftentimes i have to drag my mouse cursor and copy and paste large chunks of text to a ms word document and then ipload it to coral another issue is that coral will sometimes give extremely shprt answers this is usually because tbe question i gave was too broad and had too many factors if you need a deeper answer follow up with a more specific question the ai is really helpful for digging deeper and giving more specific answers overall coral is worth its money in my opinion however if there s a better ai out there with less document hassle i might switch over note one thing to remember is that coral ai will not write you a pristine essay it will help break down hard questions and answer them for you with references for you to add in your assignments i have to do the work by taking bits and pieces of the information coral vives me and compiling it into my assignment sometimes i copy and paste what coral writes but other times i will take an answer from coral and rewrite it my way']

Name: 114_data_data scientist_scientist_ml
Representative_Docs: ['a data scientist s day to day usually involves cleaning and analyzing data building models and translating insights into actionable decisions while some work touches ai technologies a lot of the role is focused on understanding patterns in data and communicating findings rather than building advanced ai systems like neural networks or nlp models if your goal is to dive deep into ai or machine learning a good starting point is a structured course like this article it covers the fundamentals of data science step by step with hands on exercises that give you a solid foundation for exploring ai computer vision or nlp in the future', 'they tend to be taught separately data scientists tend to run their code within frameworks e g jupyter or tableau without deploying a website data engineering may be a good field to look into that tends to merge backend development devops and data science data engineers tend to build the pipelines to get the findings of data scientists working in production for users or they create tooling for data scientists to access data from production analytics set up data architecture and tooling to make their lives easier like ways to do etl to remove bad data you could create a project like this yourself i think it would be a great portfolio project create a dashboard website to display insights from a data set kaggle can be a good place to download large datasets for analysis where you can also see other developer s work on those data sets you can also look up open data sets many governments now offer large data sets free and open source for people to use and investigate create a backend i d suggest python as it is the most commonly used language in data science create a dashboard frontend using whatever you want react is popular but complex for the css bootstrap has many things built in and may be easiest tailwind may now be more popular try to compartmentalise the project think of backend frontend data science and to some extent devops aka infrastructure as separate things that should be appropriately subdivided e g at least separate overall folders or git repos for backend frontend and data science', 'there are a few different roles that can be called data scientist so you really have to check what each company means when they say they need one are hiring one these are a few i have come across ds ml responsible for training machine learning models cleaning data feature engineering hyperparameter tunings commonly with xgboost as another person has said usually the expertise here is in the feature engineering and in partnering with software engineers to calculate the features quickly in prod and with product to make sure the ml is answering the correct business questions etc mle in some bigger companies are focused on training embeddings and training big ml models neural networks and are treated a bit more as software engineers the link to the business is a bit more abstracted they might do something novel like try a new type of feature or embedding or use a new loss function etc mle mlops deploy ml models into production monitor them allow for quick retraining ml rds responsible for building new types of machine learning algorithms often from more academy backgrounds and this is a rarer role sometimes called research data scientist these are the type of people that came up with llms for example but also work on improving the speed and accuracy of the tools that the above type of data scientists use to train models product data scientist used at meta but also some other big tech set metric goals help analyse a b tests and check if engineering launches are stat sig work closely with product to set direction do a lot of pie charts and box plots and things as well input to presentations about strategy the expertise here is about using data to make better decisions clearly some people in this forum think it s a bit low value but checking whether the product decisions companies want to take are sensible by using data before they take the decisions can actually save make lots of money these people generally aren t responsible for ml sometimes data scientist is also used for dashboard building reports building i would say this is a straight up role misnomer but this happens commonly enough that people need to watch out for it if applying for a job']

development etc recently i joined the software development team of a small to medium company different to my previous employers the company isn t a software company but has a development team for internal stuff i wouldn t say that i know the holy grail of software development but the way we plan and structure our work has me constantly raising my eye brows i need some opinions whether something like this is normal in this kind of environment there are members in my team including a project manager we are a mix of cs graduates and physicists turned developers bachelors masters i am hired as a software developer and i don t mind doing any kind of tasks but the common theme in our team is that everyone needs to be able to do everything even if for example a colleague has never worked with python servers we hardly start new tools even though the company needs them because no one is allowed to start a project without the help from others i need to include everyone in the project because working solo means we aren t pulling at the same string they fear the bus factor in my opinion unwarranted because it s not that our problems are very complicated and we should always know what the other team members are doing everything must go through a pr but are often reviewed by colleagues who never worked with your framework libraries to illustrate this there are differences we work on a task they must be prepared for others this means exactly describing what kind of change you need to do up to the line of code that needs to be modified so that theoratically everyone can
